reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary

The paper presents the Extrapolated Cyclic Primal-Dual Algorithm (ECyclicPDA), a novel coordinate descent-like method for solving two-player zero-sum extensive-form games (EFGs). The key innovation is the ability to perform pseudo block-wise updates on the sequence-form polytope, a non-separable feasible set that has historically resisted coordinate descent approaches. The method exploits the recursive structure of proximal updates induced by dilated regularizers to enable these block-wise updates while respecting the treeplex ordering of decision points. Additionally, the authors introduce an adaptive restarting heuristic that demonstrates empirical speedups across multiple EFG-solving algorithms. The paper provides theoretical convergence guarantees of O(1/T) to Nash equilibrium, notably without polynomial dependence on the number of blocks—a significant improvement over typical cyclic coordinate methods.

## Strengths

The paper makes several noteworthy theoretical and practical contributions to the problem of large-scale EFG solving. The ECyclicPDA algorithm introduces a novel and effective solution technique that addresses a previously open problem: extending cyclic coordinate descent methods to the non-separable constraints of sequence-form polytopes. This is a genuine technical achievement, as the authors demonstrate how to leverage the dilated regularizer framework in a fundamentally new way to enable block-wise updates while maintaining feasibility at the end of each full cycle.

The theoretical analysis is rigorous and provides important insights into the structure of the problem. Theorem 3.2 establishes an O(1/T) convergence rate with no dependence on the number of blocks in the ℓ1 setting, which matches the optimal bound of full gradient methods like mirror prox. This is a significant theoretical result, as Song and Diakonikolas [39] (upon which this work builds) incurred polynomial dependence on the number of blocks. The authors' specific choices of extrapolation sequences (x̃_k and ỹ_k) that leverage partial information from the current cycle are technically novel and appear to be essential for achieving this improved bound. The removal of non-standard Lipschitz assumptions from prior work further strengthens the theoretical contribution.

The paper provides a comprehensive examination of related work on coordinate descent methods and first-order techniques for EFG solving. The authors clearly articulate the distinctions between their approach and prior work, particularly explaining why the CODER algorithm of Song and Diakonikolas [39] cannot be directly applied to their setting and what additional technical innovations were necessary. The discussion of how their method differs from CODER—specifically regarding non-separability of the feasible set, the extension to general normed spaces beyond Euclidean, and the specialized extrapolation steps unique to the bilinear EFG problem—demonstrates careful technical understanding.

The empirical evaluation includes diverse block construction strategies (single block, children, postorder, and infosets), which provides useful insights into when and how block-based updates offer benefits. The experiments on four standard benchmark games (Goofspiel, Liar's Dice, Leduc, and Battleship) show that ECyclicPDA generally outperforms mirror prox, which is encouraging for a first-order method.

The restarting heuristic is an interesting practical contribution that is theoretically motivated by the sharpness property of bilinear saddle-point problems. The observation that restarting can provide substantial speedups not only for ECyclicPDA but also for existing methods like CFR+ and PCFR+ is noteworthy and suggests broader applicability of this technique. The empirical demonstration that restarting can induce linear convergence rates for CFR+ and PCFR+ on certain games is particularly intriguing and suggests deeper structure in these algorithms that deserves further investigation.

## Weaknesses

### Computational Efficiency Analysis

The most significant weakness of the paper is the insufficient analysis of the actual computational efficiency of ECyclicPDA compared to other state-of-the-art approaches. While the authors claim that the runtime of ECyclicPDA is independent of the number of blocks and that per-iteration complexity matches mirror prox, this claim is not thoroughly validated. The paper states in Section 3 that "the per-iteration complexity of our algorithm matches that of MP," but this assertion relies primarily on the implementation-specific Algorithm 2 in Appendix D and informal arguments about which rows and columns of the payoff matrix are accessed.

The wall-clock time experiments in Table 1 show only milliseconds per iteration without sufficient context about what constitutes a fair comparison. The table presents average wall-clock time per iteration but does not clearly establish whether one iteration of ECyclicPDA is truly equivalent to one iteration of MP in terms of computational work. More specifically, the claim that ECyclicPDA performs "one gradient computation and one prox computation per player" needs more rigorous justification, particularly given that the algorithm cycles through s blocks before completing a full iteration. While the authors argue that the gradient computation across all blocks still only accesses each row/column of M once, the overhead of managing partial updates, computing ĥ_k and ĝ_k, and the top-to-bottom pass to recover feasible iterates could introduce non-trivial constants.

Furthermore, the wall-clock time comparison in Table 3 shows that on Leduc (13 ranks), MP times out while ECyclicPDA reaches 10^-4 duality gap in 3.93 seconds. However, this could reflect implementation differences, hardware efficiency, or the specific stepsize tuning choices rather than fundamental algorithmic superiority. The paper would benefit from more detailed computational complexity analysis, including explicit operation counts and discussion of memory access patterns.

### Limited Empirical Advantage on Most Games

A critical limitation is that ECyclicPDA demonstrates clear superiority over CFR+ and PCFR+ only on the Battleship game. On Liar's Dice, the paper states that "ECyclicPDA is overtaking CFR+ at 10,000 gradient computations," but this is at the boundary of the experimental range, and it's unclear whether ECyclicPDA would ultimately achieve better final performance. On Goofspiel and Leduc, ECyclicPDA is not competitive with PCFR+. This narrow empirical advantage raises questions about the practical applicability of the method.

The paper acknowledges this limitation in the Discussion section: "both our block-coordinate method and restarting have a much bigger advantage in some numerical instances (Battleship, Liar's Dice) than others (Leduc and Goofspiel); a crucial question is to understand what type of game structure drives this behavior." This is an important open question, but the lack of insight into when and why ECyclicPDA should be preferred limits the practical impact of the work. The authors provide no clear guidance to practitioners about which games or game structures would benefit most from their approach.

### Incomplete Analysis of Restarting Heuristic

While the restarting heuristic is interesting, its theoretical justification is limited and somewhat disconnected from the main algorithmic contribution. The authors note that restarting is "theoretically supported by the fact that BSPPs possess the sharpness property," and cite work showing linear convergence under sharpness for ℓ2-based FOMs. However, the paper then applies restarting as a heuristic to methods using entropy and other dilated regularizers, for which no linear convergence guarantees exist. The authors themselves acknowledge this gap: "An open question raised by our work is understanding what makes restarting work for methods used with regularizers besides the ℓ2 regularizer."

The restarting rule (restart when duality gap halves since last restart) appears somewhat arbitrary. The paper provides no ablation studies on this choice—for instance, what happens if the threshold is changed to 1.5x or 2x improvement? How sensitive is the performance to this parameter? Without such analysis, it's unclear whether restarting is a fundamental technique or simply a tuning trick that happens to work well on the tested games.

### Incomplete Comparison with Related Methods

The paper mentions DCFR (Discounted CFR) in the related work discussion but does not include empirical comparisons with this method. Given that the paper emphasizes being "the first time that a FOM has been observed to outperform CFR+ on non-trivial EFGs," it would be important to compare against other recent CFR variants that might also exhibit competitive performance. The paper's claim about being first would be stronger with a more comprehensive empirical comparison.

Additionally, the paper does not discuss or compare against recent work on last-iterate convergence in EFGs (referenced in Appendix A, e.g., Lee et al. [27], Liu et al. [31]). While the authors argue that last-iterate methods are "of greater theoretical significance than practical significance," some discussion of the practical performance of these methods on the benchmark games would strengthen the empirical evaluation.

### Clarification Needed on "Nice" Regularizers

On page 5, the paper assumes access to "nice" strongly convex functions, defined as having "gradients and the gradients
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The proposed Extrapolated Cyclic Primal-Dual Algorithm (ECyclicPDA) is a solution technique for large-scale extensive-form games (EFG) that resembles first-order coordinate descent. To enable pseudo block-wise updates, it takes use of the recursive nature of the proximal update caused by dilated regularizers. A restarting heuristic for EFG solution is also presented by the authors, and it has the potential to significantly speed up both their cyclic technique as well as other current methods like mirror prox and (predictive) CFR+.

Strengths:
In summary, the ECyclicPDA algorithm introduces a novel and effective solution technique for tackling large-scale sequential games, demonstrating superior performance compared to current state-of-the-art techniques in some scenarios. The paper examines prior research on coordinate descent methods and first-order techniques for solving EFGs, highlighting the originality and benefits of their proposed ECyclicPDA approach.

Weaknesses:
The execution times and computational demands of the ECyclicPDA method in comparison to other cutting-edge approaches are not thoroughly analyzed in the paper. It is essential to evaluate such a comparison in order to ascertain the proposed algorithm's applicability and practical effectiveness. Although it is mentioned that the runtime of ECyclicPDA is independent of the number of blocks, a comprehensive evaluation is still lacking. Additionally, the paper overlooks competitive alternatives like DCFR, despite making references to these methods.

Questions:
1) The authors claim, "For the first time, a first-order method has surpassed CFR+ in performance on non-trivial EFGs." However, it seems that in the paper titled "Equivalence Analysis between Counterfactual Regret Minimization and Online Mirror Descent" by Liu et al., the authors also demonstrate instances where first-order OMD methods can outperform CFR+.

2) How strong is the assumption that the strongly convex functions are nice, see page 5, paragraph  "Dilated Regularizers"?

3) In the appendix, the last three figures share the same caption. Do these plots refer to linear, quadratic, and uniform averaging?

Limitations:
During the experiments, ECyclicPDA demonstrated superior performance compared to CFR+ solely in the Battleship game. This stands as the only instance where the results were competitive with PCFR+, which still remains the overall best-performing strategy. However, it is not entirely clear from the overall text why ECyclicPDA outperformed CFR+ specifically in the Battleship game.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

The authors motivate and explain their idea and experimentally evaluate its performance in terms of primal-dual gap in four different games. The method features a favorable theoretical convergence rate but the empirical results are often comparable or worse than existing methods in terms of the duality gap.

- The plots lack error bars (unless no variance is possible to obtain).

# Expanded Review: Block-Coordinate Methods and Restarting for Solving Extensive-Form Games

## Summary

The paper introduces the Extrapolated Cyclic Primal-Dual Algorithm (ECyclicPDA), a novel block-coordinate descent method designed specifically for solving two-player zero-sum extensive-form games (EFGs). The core contribution is the first cyclic coordinate descent approach that exploits the recursive structure of sequence-form polytopes through dilated regularizers, achieving an O(1/T) convergence rate to Nash equilibrium without polynomial dependence on the number of blocks. Additionally, the authors propose an adaptive restarting heuristic for EFG solving and provide extensive empirical evaluation across four benchmark games. The method is theoretically well-motivated and the paper is clearly presented, though the practical advantages over existing methods remain somewhat limited.

## Strengths

**1. Novel Theoretical Contribution with Clear Motivation**

The paper makes a genuine theoretical advance by extending cyclic coordinate descent methods to the non-separable constraint structure of sequence-form polytopes. The authors clearly motivate why this is non-trivial: standard coordinate descent methods require separability of the feasible set (a Cartesian product structure), but the sequence-form polytope has inherent dependencies where decisions at earlier nodes constrain all subsequent nodes. The use of dilated regularizers to enable bottom-up "pseudo-block" updates is an elegant solution to this structural challenge. The convergence rate of O(1/T) without polynomial dependence on the number of blocks (in contrast to most prior cyclic methods) represents a meaningful theoretical improvement.

**2. Excellent Presentation and Accessibility**

The paper is exceptionally well-written with clear exposition of complex concepts. The introduction successfully conveys the problem to readers unfamiliar with EFG solving by first explaining the appeal of coordinate descent methods (sparse updates, efficiency) and then articulating the specific challenge they pose for EFGs. The preliminaries section (Section 2) is thorough and well-organized, with careful notation introduced for treeplexes and dilated regularizers. The use of the Kuhn poker example in Appendix B effectively illustrates the abstract concepts. The algorithmic description in Algorithm 1 is clear, and the authors provide both a high-level version and an implementation-specific version (Algorithm 2) to demonstrate computational efficiency.

**3. Rigorous Theoretical Analysis**

The convergence proof in Theorem 3.2 is technically sound and demonstrates careful handling of the non-separable structure. The key innovation lies in Proposition 3.1, which decomposes the relaxed gap into telescoping terms, and the subsequent use of Lemma C.1 to bound error terms arising from partial updates. The proof technique—leveraging the fact that normalized entries from the same information set form a probability simplex—is specific to the EFG structure and shows deep engagement with the problem domain. The removal of nonstandard Lipschitz assumptions used in prior work (Song and Diakonikolas [39]) further strengthens the theoretical contribution.

**4. Comprehensive Experimental Validation of Design Choices**

The authors provide extensive ablation studies examining multiple dimensions of their algorithm design. Figure 1 and the associated block construction strategy comparisons (Appendix F, Figures 6-14) systematically evaluate different partitioning schemes (single block, children, postorder, infosets). This is valuable because it demonstrates that the choice of block construction strategy genuinely matters for some games (Liar's Dice, Battleship) while being inconsequential for others (Goofspiel, Leduc), and the paper documents this variation rather than hiding it. The experiments with different regularizers (dilated entropy, dilatable global entropy, dilated ℓ2) and averaging schemes (uniform, linear, quadratic) further validate the robustness of the approach across different instantiations.

**5. Novel Restarting Heuristic with Surprising Empirical Results**

The introduction of adaptive restarting—resetting the averaging process when the duality gap halves—is presented as a general heuristic rather than a theoretically guaranteed technique (except for ℓ2 regularizers where sharpness-based arguments apply). Despite this theoretical limitation, the empirical results are striking. The observation that restarted CFR+ achieves linear convergence on certain games (Figure 4, Liar's Dice) is noteworthy and suggests that restarting may be revealing underlying problem structure not previously exploited. The fact that restarted ECyclicPDA can occasionally outperform CFR+ on some games represents the first time a first-order method has achieved this on non-trivial EFGs, which is a meaningful empirical milestone.

## Weaknesses

**1. Empirical Performance Gap with State-of-the-Art Methods**

While the theoretical contributions are solid, the empirical results reveal a consistent pattern: ECyclicPDA is regularly outperformed by Predictive CFR+ (PCFR+), particularly in the non-restarted setting (Figure 2). In Goofspiel (4 ranks), ECyclicPDA and MP perform comparably, with neither approaching PCFR+'s convergence rate. In Leduc (13 ranks), ECyclicPDA shows similar or slightly worse performance than MP, and both are substantially behind PCFR+. While the paper acknowledges that CFR+ variants have inferior O(1/√T) theoretical rates, the authors do not provide a fully satisfying explanation for why their O(1/T) method consistently underperforms in practice. The paper states that CFR+ is "the fastest approach for essentially every EFG setting" and the experiments largely confirm this, which somewhat undermines the practical motivation for the proposed method.

The restarting results (Figure 4) partially mitigate this concern, as restarted ECyclicPDA becomes competitive on some games and occasionally surpasses PCFR+. However, even with restarting, the advantage is game-dependent and not universal. For instance, in Goofspiel with restarting, PCFR+ still converges to numerical precision more rapidly than ECyclicPDA. This suggests that the practical benefits of the method are limited to specific game structures (likely those where blocking provides advantages), rather than being broadly applicable.

**2. Limited Analysis of Computational Cost in Wall-Clock Time**

The paper primarily reports convergence in terms of "gradient computations" (counting one gradient evaluation for each player as two total computations). While the authors argue in Appendix D that their per-iteration complexity matches Mirror Prox (MP), actual wall-clock time measurements are relegated to Table 1, which reports average time per iteration in milliseconds. These numbers are helpful but insufficient for a complete assessment because:

- The time per iteration varies significantly across games (from 1.33 ms for ECyclicPDA on Goofspiel to 83.45 ms for PCFR+ on Battleship), and it is unclear whether this reflects implementation efficiency or fundamental algorithmic differences.
- The number of iterations to reach a given duality gap varies across methods, so time-per-iteration alone does not determine wall-clock time to convergence.
- Table 3 provides wall-clock times to reach 10^-4 duality gap, but this specific threshold may not be equally relevant for all applications. A more complete picture would show convergence curves in wall-clock time (rather than gradient computations) for multiple target precisions.
- The paper does not discuss whether the implementations are equally optimized. If ECyclicPDA's implementation is less mature than CFR+, this could artificially inflate its wall-clock time relative to its theoretical efficiency.

The authors address this in their rebuttal by noting that Table 1 shows competitive per-iteration runtimes, but the fundamental issue remains: practitioners care about wall-clock time to a desired accuracy, not theoretical gradient complexity.

**3. Absence of Variance Estimates and Error Bars**

The convergence plots (Figures 1-4, and extensive appendices) present single runs without error bars, confidence intervals, or discussion of variance. This raises several concerns:

- If the games are deterministic (which they appear to be, given the sequence-form formulation), then multiple runs should be identical. However, if there is any stochasticity in the algorithms (e.g., in initialization or block ordering), the variance is not reported.
- Even for deterministic algorithms, numerical precision and floating-point effects can introduce small variations. The paper does not discuss whether results are stable across multiple runs or hardware platforms.
- The absence of error bars makes it difficult to assess whether apparent differences between methods (e.g., ECyclicPDA vs. MP) are statistically meaningful or within noise margins.
- For the restarting experiments, the restarting triggers depend on the duality gap, which could be sensitive to numerical precision. The paper does not discuss how sensitive the results are to the restarting threshold (currently set at "halving the gap").

This is a relatively minor issue given the deterministic nature of the algorithms, but it represents a gap in experimental rigor.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper introduces a novel method for solving Extensive-Form Games based on a block-coordinate approach. The authors motivate and explain their idea and experimentally evaluate its performance in terms of primal-dual gap in four different games. The method features a favorable theoretical convergence rate but the empirical results are often comparable or worse than existing methods in terms of the duality gap.

Strengths:
- The paper is well written and free of grammar and stylistic issues.
- The introduction to the problem is very approachable even to domain non-experts.
- Experimental validation of the block construction strategy choice.
- Favorable converge properties.

Weaknesses:
- It seems that in empirical studies the method is consistently worse than PCFR+ (Fig 2 and 4). I have a difficulty to find a complete justification for this in the paper.
- Only a theoretical convergence rate and not an actual computation cost measured in units of time is reported.
- The plots lack error bars (unless no variance is possible to obtain).
- The contributions are explained indirectly. They could be listed in a more compact and more explicit form (e.g., a numbered list) to allow for critical assessment.

Questions:
- How does the run-time of the tested methods compare in practice?

*** Rebuttal Acknowledgment ***

I have read the author's rebuttal. The answer covers my questions well and the explanation the authors provides logical justification of the potential shortcomings. Therefore, I am of the opinion that the paper is sound and I increased my rating accordingly. I do not go higher due to my very limited familiarity with the subfield.

Limitations:
- The discussion of limitation is not very thorough and it is mostly limited to a mention of a lack of understanding of the game type effect on the performance.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review: CP-SLAM: Collaborative Neural Point-based SLAM

## Summary

This paper presents CP-SLAM, a collaborative neural implicit SLAM system designed for RGB-D image sequences. The authors propose a novel neural point-based 3D scene representation where each point maintains a learnable neural feature for scene encoding and is associated with a specific keyframe. A key contribution is the introduction of a distributed-to-centralized learning strategy that improves consistency and cooperation among multiple agents through federated learning of shared MLPs. Additionally, the paper presents a global optimization framework inspired by traditional bundle adjustment, incorporating pose graph optimization and keyframe-centric map refinement to improve system accuracy. The system supports both single-agent operation with loop closure and multi-agent collaborative mapping.

## Strengths

**1. Novel Neural Point-based Representation with Keyframe Association**

The proposed neural point-based representation is a thoughtful design choice that addresses a significant limitation of existing neural implicit SLAM methods. Unlike voxel grid-based approaches (e.g., NICE-SLAM, Vox-Fusion) which suffer from misalignment issues during sub-map fusion, the point-based representation allows neural points to be easily adjusted when camera poses are optimized during loop closure and pose graph optimization. This is elegantly illustrated in Figure 2, where the authors demonstrate that point clouds can be freely fused without the geometric constraints that plague grid-based methods. The keyframe-centric design (Figure 4) further enables intuitive refinement of point cloud locations following global pose graph optimization, making the approach fundamentally more suitable for collaborative SLAM scenarios.

**2. Well-Motivated Two-Stage Federated Learning Strategy**

The distributed-to-centralized learning approach (Section 3.3 and Figure 3) represents a principled solution to the collaborative mapping problem. The strategy is well-motivated: in the first stage, each agent maintains independent decoders for its own sequence, allowing local optimization without interference. After sub-map fusion via loop detection, the approach leverages federated learning to average and fine-tune shared MLPs across all sequences. This design naturally encourages consistency and cooperation while maintaining computational efficiency. The ablation study results demonstrate that this strategy is effective, with the two-agent experiments showing measurable improvements in tracking accuracy.

**3. Complete SLAM Pipeline with Traditional Back-End Integration**

Unlike recent neural implicit SLAM methods (e.g., NICE-SLAM, Vox-Fusion, NeRF-SLAM) that typically function as pure visual odometry systems without loop closure capabilities, CP-SLAM provides a more complete system. The integration of loop detection (Section 3.2), pose graph optimization (Section 3.4), and global map refinement demonstrates a thoughtful engineering effort to bridge neural implicit representations with traditional SLAM methodologies. The use of NetVLAD for loop detection and the application of Levenberg-Marquardt optimization for pose graph optimization show that the authors have carefully selected proven techniques from the SLAM literature.

**4. Strong Experimental Results on Synthetic Benchmarks**

The experimental results demonstrate clear advantages over recent neural SLAM baselines. In single-agent experiments (Table 2), CP-SLAM achieves superior tracking accuracy compared to NICE-SLAM and Vox-Fusion across all tested scenes, with average RMSE of 0.46cm compared to 2.12cm and 0.99cm respectively. The two-agent collaborative experiments (Table 1) show consistent improvements, with average improvements of approximately 13% in positioning accuracy when pose graph optimization is enabled. The map reconstruction quality (Table 4) also demonstrates superiority, with depth L1 loss of 0.38cm compared to 1.38cm for NICE-SLAM and 0.94cm for Vox-Fusion.

**5. Comprehensive Ablation Studies**

The paper includes thorough ablation studies examining key design choices. The analysis of sampling concentration (Figure 8b) provides valuable insights into why the point-based representation outperforms grid-based alternatives, showing that accuracy degrades significantly when sampling points diverge from the surface. The neural point density analysis (Table 5) demonstrates that the choice of grid cell size (ρ=14cm) is well-justified through empirical validation across scenes of varying complexity.

## Weaknesses

**1. Limited Dataset Diversity for Evaluation**

A significant limitation is that the primary evaluation relies heavily on the synthetic Replica dataset. While the authors do extend experiments to the TUM-RGBD real-world dataset (Table 3), the evaluation is limited to only three sequences (fr1-desk, fr2-xyz, fr3-office). For a collaborative SLAM system, it would be substantially more convincing to see multi-agent experiments on real-world data. The paper would benefit from evaluation on additional real-world collaborative datasets or at least more sequences from TUM-RGBD. Furthermore, the authors generate their own synthetic datasets based on Replica scenes rather than using established benchmarks with standardized evaluation protocols. This makes it difficult to compare with future work and limits the reproducibility of the experimental setup. The lack of evaluation on other popular RGB-D datasets such as ScanNet or ICL-NUIM raises questions about generalization beyond the Replica environment.

**2. Incomplete Analysis of Neural Rendering Techniques**

The paper employs relatively standard neural rendering techniques based on volume rendering (Section 3.1). The radiance MLP (U), occupancy MLP (G), and feature transformation MLP (C) follow conventional designs without significant innovation. The authors use inverse distance-based weighting for feature interpolation (Equation 4) and occupancy interpolation (Equation 6), but do not explore or justify why this particular interpolation scheme was chosen over alternatives such as learned attention mechanisms or other spatial weighting strategies. More sophisticated neural rendering techniques could potentially improve rendering quality and tracking robustness. For instance, the paper does not discuss how the method handles view-dependent effects or specular reflections, which are known challenges in neural rendering. The choice to use only geometric loss during tracking (Equation 10) while excluding photometric loss is pragmatic but not thoroughly justified—an analysis of why color information destabilizes pose estimation would strengthen the methodology.

**3. Inconsistent Performance Across Scenes**

While the paper demonstrates state-of-the-art results on average, performance varies significantly across individual scenes. For example, in Table 2, the method achieves 0.37cm RMSE on Office-3-loop but 0.56cm on Office-0-loop, and 0.44cm on Room-1-loop. The paper provides no analysis of why certain scenes are more challenging than others or what scene properties correlate with performance degradation. Similarly, in the two-agent experiments (Table 1), the performance on Apartment-0 (average RMSE of 0.95cm) is notably worse than on Office-0-C (0.65cm), yet the authors offer no explanation for this discrepancy. Understanding these failure modes would be valuable for practitioners and would strengthen the paper's contribution. The authors briefly mention in the conclusion that their method has "slightly weaker hole-filling ability in unobserved regions," but this deserves more detailed analysis with supporting evidence.

**4. Computational Requirements and Scalability Concerns**

The paper acknowledges in the conclusion that "one limitation of our method is its requirement for considerable GPU resources to operate multiple image sequences," yet provides limited analysis of these constraints. Table 6 shows runtime comparisons, but the analysis is superficial. For two-agent experiments, the system requires two RTX3090 GPUs (one per agent plus one central server), which represents significant computational overhead. The paper does not discuss how the system scales to scenarios with more than two agents or provide guidance on computational requirements for different numbers of agents. The mapping time of 10.10s per frame (Table 6) is substantially slower than Vox-Fusion's 0.36s, raising practical concerns about real-time applicability. While the authors mention that feature anchored on neural points don't need retraining after sub-map fusion, allowing iteration reduction from 200 to 150 steps, this modest improvement doesn't adequately address the fundamental computational burden.

**5. Unclear Attribution of Contributions and Missing Citations**

The paper combines multiple established techniques without always clearly delineating what is novel versus what is adapted from prior work. For instance, the rendering pipeline (volume rendering, ray sampling, MLP-based decoding) closely follows NeRF [26] and Point-NeRF [43], but the specific adaptations and differences are not always clearly explained. Section 3.1 on mapping and tracking uses similar optimization strategies to NICE-SLAM, yet the paper provides limited discussion of how the approach differs. The loop detection module uses NetVLAD [1], a pre-trained model from 2016, without exploring whether more recent place recognition methods might be beneficial. The pose graph optimization (Section 3.4) directly applies the Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm without discussing why this classical approach was preferred over more recent optimization techniques. While the paper cites relevant works, a more explicit comparison of methodological choices against alternatives would strengthen the contribution narrative.

**6. Limited Analysis

CP-SLAM incorporates neural point-based odometry, loop detection, sub-map fusion, and global refinement. Additionally, the authors propose a novel neural point 3D scene representation with keyframes to enhance map fusion and adjustment. They introduce innovative learning and optimization frameworks to ensure accurate and consistent 3D mapping for cooperative localization and mapping. The performance of CP-SLAM is evaluated on various indoor RGB-D sequences, showcasing its superior mapping and camera tracking capabilities. The contributions made by the authors significantly advance the field of SLAM and offer state-of-the-art solutions.

2. CP-SLAM retains the front-end and back-end modules like traditional SLAM systems, providing a comprehensive pipeline.
3. CP-SLAM outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both localization and reconstruction. The CP-SLAM system supports both single-agent and multi-agent modes, allowing for versatile evaluation.

CP-SLAM incorporates neural point-based odometry, loop detection, sub-map fusion, and global refinement. Additionally, the authors propose a novel neural point 3D scene representation with keyframes to enhance map fusion and adjustment. They introduce innovative learning and optimization frameworks to ensure accurate and consistent 3D mapping for cooperative localization and mapping. The performance of CP-SLAM is evaluated on various indoor RGB-D sequences, showcasing its superior mapping and camera tracking capabilities. The contributions made by the authors significantly advance the field of SLAM and offer state-of-the-art solutions.

Strengths:
1. CP-SLAM introduces a novel neural point-based representation, enabling dense collaborative neural SLAM.
2. CP-SLAM retains the front-end and back-end modules like traditional SLAM systems, providing a comprehensive pipeline.
3. CP-SLAM outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both localization and reconstruction. The CP-SLAM system supports both single-agent and multi-agent modes, allowing for versatile evaluation.

6. The ablation studies are conducted to demonstrate the importance of different modules in the proposed system.

Weaknesses:

2. The comparison with traditional methods in the two-agent experiments may not fully represent the performance of other collaborative neural SLAM approaches.

reviews.text:
# Expanded Review: CP-SLAM: Collaborative Neural Point-based SLAM

## Summary

The paper presents CP-SLAM, a collaborative neural implicit SLAM system designed to work with RGB-D image sequences. The system is composed of complete front-end and back-end modules including odometry, loop detection, sub-map fusion, and global refinement. A key innovation is the novel neural point-based 3D scene representation, where each point maintains a learnable neural feature for scene encoding and is associated with a specific keyframe. To enable effective collaboration across multiple agents, the authors propose a distributed-to-centralized learning strategy that improves consistency and cooperation between agents. Additionally, the paper introduces a novel global optimization framework inspired by traditional bundle adjustment to improve system accuracy. Extensive experiments on various datasets demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method in both camera tracking and dense mapping reconstruction. The contributions made by the authors significantly advance the field of neural SLAM and offer state-of-the-art solutions for both single-agent and multi-agent scenarios.

## Strengths

1. **Novel neural point-based representation with keyframe association**: CP-SLAM introduces an innovative neural point-based representation that is fundamentally different from prior work. Unlike feature grid-based methods (e.g., NICE-SLAM, Vox-Fusion) that struggle with coordinate system alignment during map fusion, the keyframe-centric design allows neural points to be easily adjusted when camera poses are optimized. This design choice elegantly solves a critical problem in collaborative neural SLAM that previous approaches have not adequately addressed. The association of each point with a keyframe provides a natural way to handle pose updates, similar to how traditional SLAM systems manage 3D points.

2. **Comprehensive SLAM pipeline with traditional modules**: CP-SLAM retains the front-end and back-end modules characteristic of traditional SLAM systems (odometry, loop detection, pose graph optimization), providing a complete and principled pipeline. This is a significant advantage over recent neural SLAM methods (such as NICE-SLAM and Vox-Fusion) which typically function as pure visual odometry systems without loop closure capabilities. The incorporation of pose graph optimization (Section 3.4) and global map refinement demonstrates a mature understanding of SLAM system design. The authors' explicit attention to cumulative error reduction through pose graph optimization shows they have carefully considered practical deployment scenarios where drift accumulation is a critical concern.

3. **Superior performance across multiple evaluation metrics**: CP-SLAM demonstrates state-of-the-art performance in both single-agent and multi-agent scenarios. In single-agent experiments with loop closure (Table 2), the method achieves an average RMSE of 0.46cm compared to 0.99cm for Vox-Fusion and 1.27cm for NICE-SLAM—a substantial improvement. In two-agent collaborative scenarios (Table 1), the method consistently outperforms traditional approaches like CCM-SLAM, Swarm-SLAM, and ORB-SLAM3 across multiple scenes. The reconstruction quality (Table 4) also shows advantages, with depth L1 loss of 0.38cm on average versus 0.94cm for Vox-Fusion and 1.38cm for NICE-SLAM. These quantitative results are supported by qualitative visualizations (Figures 5, 6, 7) that clearly demonstrate superior trajectory smoothness and geometric detail.

4. **Versatile evaluation in both single-agent and multi-agent modes**: The system's ability to operate in both single-agent mode with loop closure and multi-agent collaborative mode demonstrates versatility and comprehensive system design. This dual capability is important because it shows the authors have not sacrificed single-agent performance for collaborative capabilities. The evaluation protocol is well-designed, with separate experiments addressing different aspects of the system's functionality. Single-agent experiments validate the core tracking and mapping modules, while multi-agent experiments specifically evaluate the collaborative components including sub-map fusion and distributed-to-centralized learning.

5. **Realistic evaluation on Replica dataset with comprehensive metrics**: The paper uses the Replica dataset, which provides high-quality RGB-D rendering with ground truth depth and color information. This enables rigorous quantitative evaluation using multiple metrics (RMSE, Mean, Median for trajectory accuracy; depth L1 loss and mesh accuracy for reconstruction). The authors employ 196 uniformly-sampled depth maps for evaluation, providing comprehensive coverage of the reconstructed scenes. The use of multiple metrics (not just RMSE) helps prevent bias from outliers, as acknowledged by the authors when they state "so as to prevent the negative impact caused by a few extreme outliers."

6. **Comparison with state-of-the-art neural implicit methods**: In single-agent experiments, CP-SLAM is compared against recent neural implicit RGB-D SLAM methods including NICE-SLAM and Vox-Fusion, which are the most directly comparable approaches. The consistent improvements over these methods across all metrics validate the effectiveness of the neural point-based representation. Furthermore, the real-world evaluation on TUM-RGBD dataset (Table 3) against Co-SLAM and ESLAM demonstrates that the method's advantages extend beyond synthetic data.

7. **Thorough ablation studies validating design choices**: The paper includes comprehensive ablation studies examining multiple components: pose graph optimization (showing 10-13% improvement), map refinement effects (Figure 8a), sampling concentration (Figure 8b), and neural point density (Table 5). These ablations are not merely included for completeness; they provide genuine insights into the system's behavior. For example, the sampling concentration ablation reveals that when sampling points diverge from the neural point locations, error increases from 0.65cm to 1.12cm RMSE—a compelling demonstration of why the point-based representation is superior to grid-based alternatives. The neural point density analysis (Table 5) shows a clear optimal range (ρ=14cm), providing practical guidance for system configuration.

8. **Implementation reproducibility and transparency**: The authors provide extensive implementation details including GPU specifications (NVIDIA RTX3090), specific parameter settings (Nnear=16, Nuni=4, λ1=0.2, r=0.15m, ρ=0.14m, etc.), network architecture details (K=8 nearest neighbors, position encoding orders), and training procedures (200 iterations for single-agent, 150 for multi-agent). These details significantly enhance reproducibility. The authors also commit to providing supplementary material with further implementation details, demonstrating a commitment to enabling future researchers to build upon this work.

## Weaknesses

1. **Significant GPU resource requirements limit practical deployment**: CP-SLAM requires considerable GPU resources to operate multiple image sequences simultaneously. The paper explicitly acknowledges this limitation in the conclusion, stating "One limitation of our method is its requirement for considerable GPU resources to operate multiple image sequences." The system requires an NVIDIA RTX3090 GPU for single-agent operation and an additional RTX3090 as a central server for multi-agent experiments. This hardware requirement substantially limits the applicability of the method to well-resourced research institutions and commercial applications. For comparison, the memory footprint of the neural field is 0.62MB (Table 6), which is reasonable, but the computational cost of tracking (10.10s per frame) and mapping (0.30s per frame) is significant. This raises questions about whether the method could be deployed on mobile robots or edge devices commonly used in practical SLAM applications. The authors do not discuss potential strategies for reducing computational requirements, such as model compression, quantization, or hierarchical processing that might enable deployment on more modest hardware.

2. **Loop closure accuracy limitations with large viewpoint changes**: The paper identifies a critical weakness: "the relative pose computation in the loop closure relies on the existing rendering-based optimization, which may be inaccurate for large viewpoint changes thus leading to drifting of map fusion." This is a fundamental limitation because loop closure is supposed to provide globally consistent constraints, yet the rendering-based pose optimization may fail precisely when it is most needed—when the viewpoint change is substantial. The authors use two similarity thresholds (λfine and λcoarse) to mitigate this issue, but this is a workaround rather than a solution. The problem stems from the non-convex nature of the color rendering loss (Equation 10), which the authors acknowledge by using only geometric loss during tracking. However, this conservative approach may miss valid loop closures or require very high initial pose estimates. The paper does not provide empirical analysis of how often large viewpoint changes cause loop closure failures, nor does it propose solutions such as coarse-to-fine pose estimation or geometric verification methods that might improve robustness.

3. **Limited comparison with collaborative neural SLAM approaches**: While the paper claims to present "the first collaborative neural implicit SLAM system," the two-agent experiments compare against traditional methods (CCM-SLAM, Swarm-SLAM, ORB-SLAM3) rather than other neural collaborative approaches. This comparison strategy, while understandable given the novelty of the work, makes it difficult to isolate the benefits of the neural representation from the benefits of the collaborative framework itself. The paper would benefit from comparisons that separate these factors. Additionally, the comparison with ORB-SLAM
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper presents CP-SLAM, a collaborative neural implicit SLAM system developed by the authors. CP-SLAM incorporates neural point-based odometry, loop detection, sub-map fusion, and global refinement. Additionally, the authors propose a novel neural point 3D scene representation with keyframes to enhance map fusion and adjustment. They introduce innovative learning and optimization frameworks to ensure accurate and consistent 3D mapping for cooperative localization and mapping. The performance of CP-SLAM is evaluated on various indoor RGB-D sequences, showcasing its superior mapping and camera tracking capabilities. The contributions made by the authors significantly advance the field of SLAM and offer state-of-the-art solutions.

Strengths:
1. CP-SLAM introduces a novel neural point-based representation, enabling dense collaborative neural SLAM.
2. CP-SLAM retains the front-end and back-end modules like traditional SLAM systems, providing a comprehensive pipeline.
3. CP-SLAM outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both localization and reconstruction. The CP-SLAM system supports both single-agent and multi-agent modes, allowing for versatile evaluation.
4. The system is evaluated using datasets based on the Replica scenes, providing a realistic and representative testing environment.
CP-SLAM is compared against recent neural implicit RGB-D SLAM methods in single-agent experiments, showcasing its performance in loop closure.
5. In the two-agent experiments, CP-SLAM is compared with a traditional method, highlighting its collaborative capabilities.
6. The ablation studies are conducted to demonstrate the importance of different modules in the proposed system.
7. The implementation details, such as GPU specifications and parameter settings, are provided, ensuring reproducibility.

Weaknesses:
1. CP-SLAM requires significant GPU resources to handle multiple image sequences. The relative pose computation in loop closure relies on existing rendering-based optimization, which may be inaccurate for large viewpoint changes and lead to map fusion drifting.
2. The comparison with traditional methods in the two-agent experiments may not fully represent the performance of other collaborative neural SLAM approaches.
3. CP-SLAM's performance is limited by its GPU resource demand, making it less suitable for resource-constrained devices or environments.
4. Currently, a large-scale experiment is not conducted, but for multi-agents, this is an important point to consider.

Questions:
1. How does CP-SLAM handle scalability in terms of the size and complexity of the datasets?
2. Can CP-SLAM be applied to real-world datasets to evaluate its performance in practical scenarios?
3. Are there any specific limitations or challenges in the implementation of CP-SLAM that need to be addressed?
4. Can Nicer-SLAM and DROID-SLAM be compared as a contrast?
5. The article mentioned "Naturally, it is expected that the neural point cloud layout should be rearranged following global pose graph optimization. However, a world-centric point cloud map obviously cannot allow such adjustment. To tackle this limitation, we propose a keyframe-centric neural point field, where each 3D point is associated with a keyframe". Can the authors better explain why a world-centric point cloud map obviously cannot allow such adjustment? 

Sincerely,

Limitations:
The limitations have been discussed in the conclusion section.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

Summary:
The authors propose a novel collaborative RGB-D SLAM pipeline, where the map is represented by an implicit field based on Point-NERF that are transformed together with their keyframes allowing pose graph optimization. They use a loop closure detection based on learned features (NetVLAD) also for sub-map alignment. They propose a central federated learning based averaging and refinement of the distributed MLPs after submap alignment.

Strengths:
The use of point based features nicely connects the generalization and compression of implicit mapping with the ability to do efficient map refinement. The results on tracking and reconstruction show a large improvement compared to existing works.

Weaknesses:
The explanations are sometimes a bit vague. E.g. line 101 what does it mean to incrementally project a pixel to a 3d location?
The initialization of system is not described well.
While the authors describe the averaging and finetuning of the MLP weights, but do not discuss if and how the point features are shared between agents.
Evaluation was only done on synthetic data.

Questions:
Why is there another local finetuning and weight averaging done after the central averaging and finetuning?
How is the finetuning done after pose graph optimization? Is it the same as for mapping e.g. eq 9?
Would it not be possible to run ORB-SLAM3 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2007.11898.pdf) in a multi-map setting using depth from RGB-D as a baseline?
Why not evaluate on real data?
How is the TSDF fusion done to generate the final mesh?
What is the average baseline between frames in the dataset?
How long are the trajectories?

reviews.text:
# Expanded Review: CP-SLAM: Collaborative Neural Point-based SLAM

## Summary

The authors propose CP-SLAM, a novel collaborative RGB-D SLAM pipeline that represents the scene using an implicit field based on Point-NeRF principles. A key innovation is the association of neural points with keyframes, enabling efficient pose graph optimization and map refinement through coordinate transformations. The system incorporates loop closure detection via learned NetVLAD features for sub-map alignment and employs a two-stage federated learning strategy to average and refine distributed MLPs after sub-map fusion. The complete system includes front-end odometry, loop detection, sub-map fusion, and global refinement modules, presenting a comprehensive approach to collaborative neural SLAM.

## Strengths

The use of point-based neural features represents an elegant solution that successfully bridges the generalization and compression capabilities of implicit mapping with the practical ability to perform efficient map refinement. This is a significant conceptual contribution that addresses a fundamental limitation of prior grid-based neural SLAM approaches. The keyframe-centric design is particularly clever, as it naturally accommodates pose adjustments from loop closure and pose graph optimization without requiring expensive global coordinate system transformations.

The experimental results demonstrate substantial improvements over existing works in both tracking and reconstruction metrics. The quantitative evaluation shows consistent gains across multiple datasets, with the system achieving average ATE RMSE improvements of approximately 10-13% over versions without pose graph optimization. The reconstruction quality, measured by depth L1 loss and mesh accuracy, also shows meaningful improvements compared to NICE-SLAM and Vox-Fusion, with the authors achieving 0.38 cm average depth L1 loss compared to 1.38 cm and 0.94 cm respectively for competing methods.

The comprehensive system design is commendable. Unlike recent neural SLAM works that function primarily as visual odometry systems, CP-SLAM implements a complete SLAM pipeline with proper loop closure, pose graph optimization, and multi-agent support. The ablation studies effectively validate the importance of individual components, particularly demonstrating that pose graph optimization reduces average positioning error by 10% in single-agent mode and 13% in collaborative mode.

The two-stage learning strategy is well-motivated and represents a thoughtful approach to the challenges of collaborative implicit SLAM. The distributed-to-centralized learning mechanism using federated averaging provides a principled way to handle multiple independent sequences while maintaining consistency across agents.

## Major Weaknesses

### Clarity and Technical Exposition

The technical explanations lack sufficient rigor in several critical areas. For instance, the description at line 101 regarding "incrementally project each central pixel of every patch to its corresponding 3D location" is vague and requires clarification. How exactly are these 3D locations determined? Are they obtained from the depth map directly, or through some form of initialization procedure? The process by which the initial 3D point positions are established is crucial for understanding the system's behavior but remains poorly explained.

The initialization procedure for the system is inadequately described. The paper mentions using M3 = 3136 pixels and 3000-5000 optimization steps for the first frame but does not explain the initialization strategy in detail. What happens if the initial frame has missing depth values in critical regions? How are the initial MLP weights chosen? These details are essential for reproducibility and understanding the system's robustness to initialization conditions.

### Feature Sharing Between Agents

While the authors describe the averaging and fine-tuning of MLP weights in Section 3.3, there is a critical gap in the discussion regarding how point features are shared between agents. The paper states that "we fuse weights from all groups of decoders and continue with lightweight fine-tuning" but does not adequately address whether and how the per-point neural features {fj} are synchronized across agents. This is a significant omission, as the consistency of these features is fundamental to achieving proper collaboration. Are the point features from different agents' observations merged, averaged, or replaced? How are conflicts resolved when multiple agents observe the same region? These questions remain unanswered.

### Evaluation Limitations

A major limitation is the exclusive reliance on synthetic data (Replica dataset) for the primary evaluation. While the authors include real-world experiments on TUM-RGBD in Section 4.2 (Table 3), these results are presented as secondary and show less impressive improvements. The real-world results reveal that the method achieves 5.20 cm average RMSE compared to 5.58 cm for Co-SLAM and fails to demonstrate the same level of superiority observed in synthetic experiments. This raises questions about the generalization of the approach to real-world scenarios with their inherent challenges: lighting variations, sensor noise, dynamic objects, and texture-less regions.

The synthetic nature of the Replica dataset may not adequately capture the challenges present in real-world SLAM, such as motion blur, rolling shutter effects, specular reflections, and significant appearance changes due to lighting variations. The paper would be significantly strengthened by more extensive real-world evaluation, particularly on standard benchmarks like ScanNet or more recent RGB-D datasets with ground truth trajectories.

## Minor Weaknesses and Questions

### Technical Details

The paper raises several unresolved technical questions. Why is there a second round of local fine-tuning and weight averaging performed after the central averaging and fine-tuning described in Section 3.3? The federated learning mechanism appears to involve multiple rounds of averaging, but the motivation and necessity of these multiple stages is unclear. Does each round provide diminishing returns? What is the convergence behavior?

The fine-tuning procedure following pose graph optimization is inadequately specified. The paper states that "we end up performing a low-cost fine-tuning of the global neural point fields with fewer optimization iterations" but does not clarify whether this uses the same loss function as Equation 9 or a modified version. How many iterations constitute "low-cost"? What are the computational costs associated with this refinement stage?

The TSDF fusion procedure for generating the final mesh is mentioned but not described in detail. The paper references the "built-in function in Open3D" but does not specify parameters such as voxel size, truncation distance, or weighting schemes. These details are important for understanding the reconstruction pipeline and for reproducibility.

### Experimental Design

Several experimental details would strengthen the evaluation. What is the average baseline (spatial separation) between consecutive frames in the Replica dataset? This information is crucial for understanding the difficulty of the tracking task and for comparing with other methods. How long are the complete trajectories? The paper mentions 1500 frames for single-agent and 2500 frames per agent for collaborative experiments, but the actual spatial extent and temporal duration are not specified.

The comparison with ORB-SLAM3 in the two-agent setting is somewhat unfair, as the authors acknowledge that ORB-SLAM3 is not designed for collaborative SLAM. While the authors use ORB-SLAM3's "atlas" strategy for multi-sequence processing, this may not represent the system's optimal configuration for this task. A more principled comparison might involve evaluating ORB-SLAM3's ability to handle multi-map scenarios with depth information, as suggested in the original questions.

### System Limitations

The paper acknowledges in Section 5 that the system requires "considerable GPU resources to operate multiple image sequences," requiring an RTX3090 per agent plus an additional server GPU for centralized processing. This is a significant practical limitation that restricts deployment to resource-rich environments. The paper would benefit from discussing potential approaches to reduce computational requirements or strategies for edge deployment.

The "slightly weaker hole-filling ability in unobserved regions" compared to feature grid-based methods is mentioned but not thoroughly investigated. While the authors explain that neural points are distributed around observed surfaces, it would be valuable to quantify this limitation and discuss potential mitigation strategies. How does this affect reconstruction quality in partially observed scenes?

The authors note that "relative pose computation in the loop closure relies on the existing rendering-based optimization, which may be inaccurate for large viewpoint changes thus leading to drifting of map fusion." This is a significant limitation that could severely impact system performance in challenging scenarios. While the authors propose designing "a lightweight system with a coarse-to-fine pose estimation for future work," this limitation warrants more thorough investigation in the current work, including failure case analysis.

## Technical Soundness

The overall technical approach is sound and well-grounded in established principles from both traditional SLAM and neural implicit representations. The volume rendering formulation (Equations 2-8) correctly adapts NeRF-style rendering to the point-based representation. The pose graph optimization (Equation 12) follows standard SLAM methodology. The federated learning approach for MLP weight averaging is a reasonable adaptation of established techniques to this domain.

However, some technical choices warrant further justification. The fixed query radius r = 0.15m and cubic cell size ρ = 0.14m appear to be chosen empirically, as confirmed by the ablation study in Table 5. While the ablation study demonstrates that these values work well across test scenes, the sensitivity to these hyperparameters and their adaptability to scenes of different scales is not thoroughly explored. Would the method require parameter tuning for significantly different scene types (e.

reviews.summary: This paper proposed a collaborative RGB-D neural implicit SLAM method named CP-SLAM. CP-SLAM is based on neural point representation similar to PointNeRF, which is natually suitable for collaborative SLAM and loop closing. Based on this neural point representation, CP-SLAM is able to perform full SLAM with a single or multiple agents: tracking, mapping, loop detection, loop closing, submap merging, etc. Experiments show that CP-SLAM achieves SOTA results compared with previous collaborative SLAM and neural implicit SLAM methods.
reviews.strengths:
1. Using neural point representation in the context of neural implicit SLAM is novel and the motivation of using it is also clear, i.e. more suitable for loop closing and sub-map merging.

2. A full SLAM system that is capable of loop closing, Pose Graph Optimisation and sub-map merging. This is the first time I saw a full SLAM system in the context of neural implicit SLAM.

3. The system seems well engineered, putting many components together and works effectively well.

4. Competitive tracking and mapping results compared with previous collaborative and neural SLAM method.
reviews.weaknesses: The motivation is clear and the method is very well engineered, but from novelty perspective, all the components in CP-SLAM are already there with some incremental modification: e.g. the scene representation is from PointNeRF, Loop detection is from an off-the-shelf NetVLAD model, etc.
reviews.questions:
1. From Sec. 3.1 it seems that at each frame a new set of neural points are created densely via back-projection, and there doesn't seem to be any point merging scheme. Then how is CP-SLAM able to achieve 0.62MB parameters size in Tab. 4?

2. Also from Sec. 3.1 it seems that the rendering loss will only optimize the neural features stored in the points but will never affect the locations of the points, which means once created the location of a point will never change unless a loop is detected and a Pose Graph Optimisation is performed. If this is the case, then the entire mapping process would be equivalent to simply back-projecting the depth images?

3. How did you convert your point-based map to mesh for evaluation in Tab. 3?

4. Following my question in 2: if CP-SLAM's map points are obtained explicitly from depth measurement, then it is supposed to have better accuracy than NICE-SLAM and Vox-Fusion (the same reason that TSDF-Fusion has better accuracy than neural SLAM methods). Thus, better accuracy numbers in Tab. 3 is not enough to prove CP-SLAM have "more powerful geometric reconstruction capability". Also it's not clear why the authors didn't report completion.

After rebuttal: the authors have addressed all my questions and concerns.
reviews.limitations: Yes
