Summary:
This paper proposed a collaborative RGB-D neural implicit SLAM method named CP-SLAM. CP-SLAM is based on neural point representation similar to PointNeRF, which is natually suitable for collaborative SLAM and loop closing. Based on this neural point representation, CP-SLAM is able to perform full SLAM with a single or multiple agents: tracking, mapping, loop detection, loop closing, submap merging, etc. Experiments show that CP-SLAM achieves SOTA results compared with previous collaborative SLAM and neural implicit SLAM methods.

Strengths:
1. Using neural point representation in the context of neural implicit SLAM is novel and the motivation of using it is also clear, i.e. more suitable for loop closing and sub-map merging.

2. A full SLAM system that is capable of loop closing, Pose Graph Optimisation and sub-map merging. This is the first time I saw a full SLAM system in the context of neural implicit SLAM.

3. The system seems well engineered, putting many components together and works effectively well.

4. Competitive tracking and mapping results compared with previous collaborative and neural SLAM method.

Weaknesses:
The motivation is clear and the method is very well engineered, but from novelty perspective, all the components in CP-SLAM are already there with some incremental modification: e.g. the scene representation is from PointNeRF, Loop detection is from an off-the-shelf NetVLAD model, etc.

Questions:
1. From Sec. 3.1 it seems that at each frame a new set of neural points are created densely via back-projection, and there doesn't seem to be any point merging scheme. Then how is CP-SLAM able to achieve 0.62MB parameters size in Tab. 4?

2. Also from Sec. 3.1 it seems that the rendering loss will only optimize the neural features stored in the points but will never affect the locations of the points, which means once created the location of a point will never change unless a loop is detected and a Pose Graph Optimisation is performed. If this is the case, then the entire mapping process would be equivalent to simply back-projecting the depth images?

3. How did you convert your point-based map to mesh for evaluation in Tab. 3?

4. Following my question in 2: if CP-SLAM's map points are obtained explicitly from depth measurement, then it is supposed to have better accuracy than NICE-SLAM and Vox-Fusion (the same reason that TSDF-Fusion has better accuracy than neural SLAM methods). Thus, better accuracy numbers in Tab. 3 is not enough to prove CP-SLAM have "more powerful geometric reconstruction capability". Also it's not clear why the authors didn't report completion.

After rebuttal: the authors have addressed all my questions and concerns.

Limitations:
Yes

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Expanded Review: CP-SLAM: Collaborative Neural Point-based SLAM

## Summary

This paper presents CP-SLAM, a collaborative RGB-D neural implicit SLAM system that introduces a novel neural point-based 3D scene representation for enabling cooperative localization and mapping across multiple agents. The system is built upon a neural point representation inspired by Point-NeRF, where each 3D point maintains a learnable neural feature anchored to a specific keyframe. The authors propose a distributed-to-centralized learning strategy to improve consistency in collaborative settings and incorporate traditional SLAM components including loop detection, pose graph optimization, and global map refinement. The system is evaluated on both single-agent scenarios with loop closure and multi-agent collaborative tasks, demonstrating state-of-the-art results compared to recent neural implicit SLAM and traditional collaborative SLAM methods.

## Strengths

1. **Clear Motivation for Neural Point Representation**: The use of neural point representation in the context of neural implicit SLAM is well-motivated and novel. Unlike existing implicit representations such as voxel grids or feature grids, the point-based approach naturally facilitates loop closure and sub-map merging because neural points can be easily adjusted and transformed when camera poses are optimized. The authors provide a compelling illustration in Figure 2 demonstrating how voxel grid-based methods suffer from misalignment issues during sub-map fusion, whereas their point-based approach allows for "free fusion" without geometric constraints. This representation choice directly addresses a fundamental limitation of prior neural SLAM methods that lack proper mechanisms for handling pose corrections and collaborative scenarios.

2. **Complete SLAM System with Full Pipeline**: This work presents the first comprehensive neural implicit SLAM system capable of handling the complete SLAM pipeline including odometry, loop detection, loop closing, pose graph optimization, and sub-map merging. Prior neural implicit SLAM methods such as NICE-SLAM and Vox-Fusion were primarily visual odometry systems without loop closure capabilities or mechanisms for multi-agent collaboration. The integration of these components into a unified framework is non-trivial and represents a significant contribution. The paper demonstrates that a neural implicit representation can be successfully integrated with traditional SLAM back-end components (pose graph optimization, loop constraints) that were previously thought incompatible with neural representations. This is a meaningful step toward making neural SLAM practical for real-world applications requiring global consistency.

3. **Well-Engineered System with Thoughtful Design Choices**: The system demonstrates solid engineering throughout its design. The distributed-to-centralized learning strategy (Section 3.3) is a clever approach to collaborative learning that leverages federated learning principles to ensure consistency across agents while maintaining computational efficiency. The keyframe-centric neural point field design (Section 3.4) elegantly solves the problem of adjusting point locations after pose graph optimization by associating each point with a keyframe rather than using world-centric coordinates. The two-stage MLP training approach—where independent decoders are maintained during the distributed stage and then merged into shared decoders after sub-map fusion—shows careful consideration of the collaborative SLAM workflow. Additionally, the grid-based filtering strategy for handling neural point redundancy after fusion demonstrates practical engineering awareness.

4. **Strong Experimental Results Across Multiple Evaluation Metrics**: CP-SLAM achieves competitive or superior tracking and mapping results compared to both neural implicit SLAM methods (NICE-SLAM, Vox-Fusion) and traditional collaborative SLAM approaches (CCM-SLAM, Swarm-SLAM, ORB-SLAM3). In single-agent loop closure experiments (Table 2), the method achieves an average RMSE of 0.46cm compared to 2.12cm for NICE-SLAM and 0.99cm for Vox-Fusion. In two-agent collaboration (Table 1), the system consistently outperforms baselines across multiple scenes. The mapping quality evaluation (Table 4) shows superior depth reconstruction accuracy and mesh quality. The ablation studies effectively demonstrate the contribution of individual components, with pose graph optimization providing a 10% improvement in single-agent and 13% improvement in two-agent scenarios.

## Weaknesses

1. **Limited Technical Novelty at Component Level**: While the system integration is commendable, the individual technical components are largely derived from existing work with incremental modifications. The neural point representation is adapted directly from Point-NeRF [43], the loop detection mechanism relies on the off-the-shelf NetVLAD [1] model without modifications, and the pose graph optimization follows standard approaches from traditional SLAM systems. The volume rendering pipeline closely follows the NeRF [26] formulation. The distributed-to-centralized learning strategy, while sensible, is a relatively straightforward application of federated learning averaging principles. From a pure novelty perspective, the primary contribution is recognizing that neural point representations are more amenable to collaborative SLAM rather than proposing fundamentally new technical innovations. The paper would be strengthened by more novel components in the learning strategy or optimization framework.

2. **Insufficient Clarity on Point Creation and Memory Efficiency Claims**: Section 3.1 describes creating neural points densely through back-projection at each frame (dividing images into 4×4 patches and projecting central pixels), yet the paper provides limited explanation of how redundancy is managed during the mapping process. The claim of achieving 0.62MB feature size (Table 6) appears inconsistent with the dense point creation strategy described. If new points are created at every frame during mapping, how does the system avoid exponential growth in the number of points? While the authors mention grid-based filtering with parameter ρ=0.14m, the paper lacks detailed analysis of how many points are typically retained across different scenes, how the filtering strategy evolves over time, and how many points are discarded versus retained. A more thorough explanation of the point lifecycle—creation, filtering, redundancy removal, and final retention—would strengthen the technical clarity. Additionally, a detailed comparison of point cloud sizes across different scenes would help readers understand the actual memory footprint of the system.

3. **Unclear Point Location Optimization During Mapping**: The paper states that rendering loss optimizes neural features (f_i) but does not explicitly explain whether point locations are optimized during the mapping process. From Section 3.1, it appears that point locations are fixed once created through back-projection and only adjusted when pose graph optimization is triggered after loop closure. If this interpretation is correct, then the mapping process is largely equivalent to back-projecting depth images and storing neural features at those locations. This raises questions about whether the system truly "learns" scene geometry or primarily encodes it from the ground truth depth input. The paper would benefit from explicit clarification on: (1) whether point locations are ever optimized during the continuous mapping phase, (2) how much of the mapping quality improvement comes from neural feature learning versus explicit depth encoding, and (3) what happens in regions where depth measurements are noisy or missing. An ablation study comparing the system with frozen point locations versus optimized locations would help quantify the contribution of point location learning.

4. **Mesh Reconstruction Evaluation Limitations**: The evaluation in Table 4 compares depth L1 loss and mesh accuracy, but the paper does not report mesh completion metrics despite acknowledging in Section 4.3 that "scenes are not completely scanned, which leads to holes in mesh reconstruction." This selective reporting is problematic because it makes it difficult to assess whether the superior accuracy numbers represent genuine geometric reconstruction improvements or simply reflect the system's reliance on explicit depth measurements. Since CP-SLAM uses ground truth depth for point creation (through back-projection), it is expected to have better accuracy than methods that infer geometry from color alone. The more meaningful comparison would be completion metrics, which would reveal whether the neural representation can effectively interpolate and extrapolate geometry in unobserved regions. The authors' explanation that completion metrics are omitted due to incomplete scene scans is insufficient—completion metrics are designed precisely to measure performance in partially observed scenes. Furthermore, the paper lacks discussion of how the point-based representation handles regions with missing or unreliable depth measurements, which is crucial for understanding the method's robustness.

5. **Limited Analysis of Scalability and Computational Requirements**: While Table 6 provides runtime and memory analysis for a single scene, the paper offers limited discussion of how the system scales with scene complexity, number of agents, and trajectory length. The requirement for "considerable GPU resources to operate multiple image sequences" (acknowledged in the conclusion) is a significant practical limitation. The paper shows results for only two-agent scenarios—does the system scale linearly to more agents? How does the computational cost of pose graph optimization grow with the number of keyframes and loop constraints? What is the memory footprint when processing longer sequences? Additionally, the paper does not discuss how the system handles dynamic scenes or provides quantitative timing breakdowns for different components (tracking, mapping, loop detection, pose graph optimization). For a system targeting multi-agent collaborative SLAM, more detailed analysis of computational scaling would strengthen the practical applicability assessment.

## Questions and Concerns

1. **Point Cloud Size and Memory Efficiency**: The paper claims 0.62MB feature size in Table 6, which seems remarkably compact given the dense point creation strategy. Could the authors provide: (a) the typical number of neural points retained in each scene, (b) how the point count
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper proposed a collaborative RGB-D neural implicit SLAM method named CP-SLAM. CP-SLAM is based on neural point representation similar to PointNeRF, which is natually suitable for collaborative SLAM and loop closing. Based on this neural point representation, CP-SLAM is able to perform full SLAM with a single or multiple agents: tracking, mapping, loop detection, loop closing, submap merging, etc. Experiments show that CP-SLAM achieves SOTA results compared with previous collaborative SLAM and neural implicit SLAM methods.

Strengths:
1. Using neural point representation in the context of neural implicit SLAM is novel and the motivation of using it is also clear, i.e. more suitable for loop closing and sub-map merging.

2. A full SLAM system that is capable of loop closing, Pose Graph Optimisation and sub-map merging. This is the first time I saw a full SLAM system in the context of neural implicit SLAM.

3. The system seems well engineered, putting many components together and works effectively well.

4. Competitive tracking and mapping results compared with previous collaborative and neural SLAM method.

Weaknesses:
The motivation is clear and the method is very well engineered, but from novelty perspective, all the components in CP-SLAM are already there with some incremental modification: e.g. the scene representation is from PointNeRF, Loop detection is from an off-the-shelf NetVLAD model, etc.

Questions:
1. From Sec. 3.1 it seems that at each frame a new set of neural points are created densely via back-projection, and there doesn't seem to be any point merging scheme. Then how is CP-SLAM able to achieve 0.62MB parameters size in Tab. 4?

2. Also from Sec. 3.1 it seems that the rendering loss will only optimize the neural features stored in the points but will never affect the locations of the points, which means once created the location of a point will never change unless a loop is detected and a Pose Graph Optimisation is performed. If this is the case, then the entire mapping process would be equivalent to simply back-projecting the depth images?

3. How did you convert your point-based map to mesh for evaluation in Tab. 3?

4. Following my question in 2: if CP-SLAM's map points are obtained explicitly from depth measurement, then it is supposed to have better accuracy than NICE-SLAM and Vox-Fusion (the same reason that TSDF-Fusion has better accuracy than neural SLAM methods). Thus, better accuracy numbers in Tab. 3 is not enough to prove CP-SLAM have "more powerful geometric reconstruction capability". Also it's not clear why the authors didn't report completion.

After rebuttal: the authors have addressed all my questions and concerns.

Limitations:
Yes

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: The paper presents a collaborative implicit neural simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) system for RGB-D image sequences, which is able to merge sub-maps into a global map and also handle loop-closure for neural mapper for the first time. To facilitate the capability of doing loop-closure and sub-map merging, the authors introduce a neural point-based 3D scene representation, where each point maintains a learnable neural feature for scene encoding and is associated with a keyframe. The optimization follows the traditional SLAM’s graph optimization fashion. The experimental results on diverse datasets demonstrate the superior performance of the proposed method in both camera tracking and mapping.
reviews.strengths:
1. Novel Neural Point-based 3D Scene Representation: A neural point cloud representation for scene encoding in the proposed framework is a significant contribution. This approach allows each point to carry encoded information, improving the system's ability to represent and cross-view association for large-scale tracking and pose refinement. 
2.Comprehensive Collaborative Framework: The authors provide a collaborative SLAM system with front-end and back-end modules, addressing essential aspects such as odometry, loop detection, sub-map fusion, and global refinement. This comprehensive approach ensures that the proposed system can handle various challenges typically encountered in SLAM tasks. 
3.Distributed-to-Centralized Learning Strategy: The incorporation of a distributed-to-centralized learning strategy is an effective solution for improving consistency and cooperation in collaborative implicit SLAM. 
4.Improved Performance: The experimental results on multiple datasets demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method in both camera tracking and mapping.
reviews.weaknesses:
1.In the Introduction: This paper emphasizes the significance of targeting collaborative-SLAM, considering the substantial value already provided by loop-closure capability in effectively reducing pose drift. However, the evaluation of the paper becomes more challenging due to the necessity for additional comparative studies that can convincingly demonstrate the advantages offered by both approaches.

2.In Section 2: For collaborative mapping, this paper should cite more recent works including:
[1*] Campos, Carlos, et al. "Orb-slam3: An accurate open-source library for visual, visual–inertial, and multimap slam." IEEE Transactions on Robotics 37.6 (2021): 1874-1890.
[2*] Tian, Yulun, et al. "Kimera-multi: Robust, distributed, dense metric-semantic slam for multi-robot systems." IEEE Transactions on Robotics 38.4 (2022).

It is also expected to provide comparative studies to the above researches.

3.In Section 3: 
(1). Line 101: This paper introduced 4x4 patches to compute the point features. However, it misses details on there is a limitation on the number of patches. What if we increase the size of the patches? Will it impact the later performance? 
(2) This paper introduced so many symbols but without giving any explanation of the symbols, which makes it really hard to follow the paper. Please revise the whole paper and make them clear to the readers.
(3) Equation 10: The explanation “Considering the strong non-convexity of the color map, only the geometry part is included in tracking loss” for not using color in the loss is not convincing. The authors are expected to provide ablation studies to prove the claim.

4.In Section 4: More experiments are expected:
(1). More comparative studies on Collaborative-SLAM are expected.
(2). For single-agent cases: The local covisibility map can be formed as well; it would be interesting to only look at cases without a loop but having a local covisibility map.
(3). Ablation study on “Distributed-to-Centralized Learning” is needed to prove why this is needed.
reviews.questions:
1.In the context of this paper, which relies on point features for establishing frame-to-frame correspondence, an important concern arises regarding the maintenance of continuous tracking. Unlike the traditional SLAM pipeline, where feature locations typically reside around corners, this paper selects points without necessarily adhering to this characteristic. Thus, it becomes crucial to address how this approach ensures the continuity of tracking throughout the SLAM process.
2.Collaborative mapping can be done by recent work on No Pose Nerf:
[]Bian, Wenjing, et al. "Nope-nerf: Optimising neural radiance field with no pose prior." Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition. 2023.
This paper should provide a comparison to show the advantage of the proposed method.
3. This paper has too many contributions, which does not form a scientific paper but more an engineering paper. Not sure if this paper fall into the NeuralIPS group.
reviews.limitations:
1. The writing should be improved, especially the equations should be better explained.
2. More experiments and ablation studies should be provided to demonstrate the advantage of the proposed algorithms. 
3. The literature review needs to refer to more recent research.

Summary:
The paper presents a collaborative implicit neural simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) system for RGB-D image sequences, which is able to merge sub-maps into a global map and also handle loop-closure for neural mapper for the first time. To facilitate the capability of doing loop-closure and sub-map merging, the authors introduce a neural point-based 3D scene representation, where each point maintains a learnable neural feature for scene encoding and is associated with a keyframe. The optimization follows the traditional SLAM’s graph optimization fashion. The experimental results on diverse datasets demonstrate the superior performance of the proposed method in both camera tracking and mapping.

Strengths:
1. Novel Neural Point-based 3D Scene Representation: A neural point cloud representation for scene encoding in the proposed framework is a significant contribution. This approach allows each point to carry encoded information, improving the system's ability to represent and cross-view association for large-scale tracking and pose refinement. 
2.Comprehensive Collaborative Framework: The authors provide a collaborative SLAM system with front-end and back-end modules, addressing essential aspects such as odometry, loop detection, sub-map fusion, and global refinement. This comprehensive approach ensures that the proposed system can handle various challenges typically encountered in SLAM tasks. 
3.Distributed-to-Centralized Learning Strategy: The incorporation of a distributed-to-centralized learning strategy is an effective solution for improving consistency and cooperation in collaborative implicit SLAM. 
4.Improved Performance: The experimental results on multiple datasets demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method in both camera tracking and mapping.

Weaknesses:
1.In the Introduction: This paper emphasizes the significance of targeting collaborative-SLAM, considering the substantial value already provided by loop-closure capability in effectively reducing pose drift. However, the evaluation of the paper becomes more challenging due to the necessity for additional comparative studies that can convincingly demonstrate the advantages offered by both approaches.

2.In Section 2: For collaborative mapping, this paper should cite more recent works including:
[1*] Campos, Carlos, et al. "Orb-slam3: An accurate open-source library for visual, visual–inertial, and multimap slam." IEEE Transactions on Robotics 37.6 (2021): 1874-1890.
[2*] Tian, Yulun, et al. "Kimera-multi: Robust, distributed, dense metric-semantic slam for multi-robot systems." IEEE Transactions on Robotics 38.4 (2022).

It is also expected to provide comparative studies to the above researches.

3.In Section 3: 
(1). Line 101: This paper introduced 4x4 patches to compute the point features. However, it misses details on there is a limitation on the number of patches. What if we increase the size of the patches? Will it impact the later performance? 
(2) This paper introduced so many symbols but without giving any explanation of the symbols, which makes it really hard to follow the paper. Please revise the whole paper and make them clear to the readers.
(3) Equation 10: The explanation “Considering the strong non-convexity of the color map, only the geometry part is included in tracking loss” for not using color in the loss is not convincing. The authors are expected to provide ablation studies to prove the claim.

4.In Section 4: More experiments are expected:
(1). More comparative studies on Collaborative-SLAM are expected.

Thus, it becomes crucial to address how this approach ensures the continuity of tracking throughout the SLAM process.
2.Collaborative mapping can be done by recent work on No Pose Nerf:
[]Bian, Wenjing, et al. "Nope-nerf: Optimising neural radiance field with no pose prior." Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition. 2023.
This paper should provide a comparison to show the advantage of the proposed method.

reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review: CP-SLAM: Collaborative Neural Point-based SLAM

## Summary

This paper introduces CP-SLAM, a collaborative implicit neural simultaneous localization and mapping system designed for RGB-D image sequences. The work represents a significant effort to extend neural implicit SLAM methods to support both multi-agent collaborative mapping and single-agent loop closure—capabilities that have been notably absent from prior neural SLAM systems. To enable these features within a unified framework, the authors propose a novel neural point-based 3D scene representation where each point maintains learnable neural features for scene encoding and is explicitly associated with a specific keyframe. The system incorporates distributed-to-centralized learning strategies for improved consistency across agents and employs traditional pose graph optimization techniques adapted for the neural implicit domain. Experimental validation on diverse datasets demonstrates competitive performance in both camera tracking accuracy and dense map reconstruction quality compared to recent neural and traditional SLAM baselines.

## Strengths

### 1. Novel Neural Point-based 3D Scene Representation

The introduction of a neural point cloud representation is a significant and well-motivated contribution to the field of neural implicit SLAM. Unlike existing approaches that rely on dense voxel grids (NICE-SLAM, Vox-Fusion) or global feature grids, the point-based representation offers distinct advantages for the collaborative SLAM setting. By anchoring learnable neural features directly to 3D points, the approach enables flexible scene representation that naturally accommodates the geometric transformations inherent in pose refinement and loop closure. As demonstrated in Figure 2, this representation avoids the misalignment issues that plague voxel-grid-based methods when coordinate systems are adjusted during sub-map fusion. The keyframe-centric design (Section 3.4) further enhances this capability by allowing neural points to be repositioned as camera poses are optimized through pose graph optimization, mirroring the behavior of traditional 3D point clouds in conventional SLAM systems. This design choice is particularly clever because it bridges the gap between the flexibility of traditional geometric representations and the expressiveness of neural implicit fields.

### 2. Comprehensive Collaborative Framework with Complete SLAM Pipeline

The paper presents what appears to be the first complete end-to-end collaborative neural SLAM system that incorporates all essential components of a traditional SLAM pipeline. The system encompasses visual odometry with differentiable volume rendering (Section 3.1), learning-based loop detection using NetVLAD descriptors (Section 3.2), sub-map fusion with alignment procedures, pose graph optimization (Section 3.4), and global map refinement. This comprehensive approach addresses multiple fundamental challenges that have limited prior neural SLAM work. The integration of loop closure detection and pose graph optimization into a neural implicit framework is non-trivial, as these operations require the ability to adjust the scene representation following pose updates—a capability that is difficult to achieve with traditional implicit representations like MLPs or dense feature grids. The system's ability to handle both single-agent scenarios (with loop closure) and multi-agent collaborative scenarios within the same framework demonstrates architectural flexibility and generalizability.

### 3. Distributed-to-Centralized Learning Strategy

The two-stage learning strategy (Section 3.3) represents a thoughtful approach to the unique challenges of collaborative neural SLAM. By initially maintaining separate decoder networks for each agent (distributed stage) and subsequently fusing and fine-tuning shared decoders across all sequences (centralized stage), the method balances computational efficiency with the need for consistent scene representation across agents. The incorporation of federated learning principles is well-motivated and provides a principled approach to knowledge sharing. This strategy is particularly valuable in collaborative settings where agents may operate asynchronously and have different computational capabilities. The lightweight fine-tuning phase following weight averaging ensures that the unified representation adequately captures the geometry and appearance information from all contributing agents without excessive retraining.

### 4. Strong Experimental Performance Across Multiple Datasets and Metrics

The experimental evaluation demonstrates consistent improvements over state-of-the-art methods across diverse scenarios. In two-agent collaborative experiments (Table 1), the method achieves notably lower trajectory errors compared to CCM-SLAM, Swarm-SLAM, and ORB-SLAM3 across multiple scenes. For single-agent loop closure scenarios (Table 2), the method outperforms both recent neural approaches (NICE-SLAM, Vox-Fusion) and the traditional ORB-SLAM3 baseline. The map reconstruction results (Table 4) further validate the approach, showing lower depth L1 loss and mesh accuracy errors. The qualitative results in Figures 6 and 7 demonstrate smoother trajectories and more detailed geometry reconstruction. Additionally, the real-world experiments on TUM-RGBD (Table 3) show that the method maintains its advantages in practical settings, which is important for validating the approach beyond synthetic datasets.

## Weaknesses

### 1. Insufficient Justification of Collaborative SLAM Motivation and Evaluation

While the paper emphasizes the significance of collaborative SLAM, the actual benefits of the collaborative approach compared to the loop-closure capability alone are not convincingly demonstrated. The paper states in the introduction that collaborative SLAM enables "cooperative localization and mapping for multiple agents," but the experimental results do not clearly isolate and quantify the improvements attributable specifically to multi-agent collaboration versus single-agent loop closure. The comparison between "ours(w/o)" and "ours(w/)" in Tables 1 and 2 shows improvements from pose graph optimization, but this is not equivalent to demonstrating the value of collaborative mapping specifically. 

More critically, the paper lacks ablation studies that would illuminate the benefits of the distributed-to-centralized learning strategy compared to simpler alternatives (e.g., training separate models for each agent without fusion, or training a single global model from the beginning). The paper would be significantly strengthened by experiments that quantify: (1) the overhead of collaborative coordination versus the gains in mapping accuracy and consistency, (2) the specific advantages of the two-stage learning approach compared to alternatives, and (3) scenarios where collaboration provides substantial benefits over single-agent operation with loop closure. Such analyses would better justify the additional complexity introduced by the collaborative framework.

### 2. Incomplete Literature Review and Missing Comparative Studies

The related work section (Section 2) omits several important recent works in collaborative and neural SLAM that should be discussed and compared against:

- **Kimera-Multi** (Tian et al., 2022): A robust, distributed, dense metric-semantic SLAM system for multi-robot systems that provides a stronger collaborative baseline than CCM-SLAM and Swarm-SLAM.
- **No-Pose NeRF** (Bian et al., 2023): A neural SLAM approach that optimizes NeRF without pose priors, which could be relevant for collaborative scenarios where pose initialization may be uncertain.
- **Recent distributed SLAM systems**: The paper could benefit from more thorough coverage of recent distributed approaches beyond the few mentioned.

The absence of comparative studies with these methods is a notable limitation. While the paper compares against ORB-SLAM3 in a somewhat artificial manner (concatenating sequences), direct comparisons with other collaborative neural approaches would strengthen the claims of novelty and superiority. The fact that "no collaborative neural SLAM work has emerged so far" (Section 4) may be true, but this actually raises the question of whether the collaborative aspect is sufficiently important to warrant the added complexity, given that the single-agent results are already competitive.

### 3. Unclear Technical Details and Insufficient Ablation Studies

#### Patch Size and Neural Point Density

The paper introduces 4×4 patches for computing point features (Section 3.1) but provides no justification for this choice or analysis of its impact. Critical questions remain unanswered: What is the sensitivity of the system to patch size? How does the number of extracted neural points scale with patch size, and what are the computational implications? While Table 5 provides an ablation on the grid filtering parameter ρ, there is no corresponding analysis for the patch resolution, which seems equally fundamental to the representation.

#### Symbol Definitions and Mathematical Clarity

Throughout Section 3, numerous symbols are introduced with insufficient explanation. For example:
- The notation in Equations 3-7 introduces several intermediate representations (f_k, f_{k,x_i}, f_{x_i}, σ) but the relationships between them and the geometric intuition could be clearer.
- The weighted interpolation scheme in Equations 4 and 6 uses inverse distance weighting, but the paper doesn't explain why this particular weighting strategy was chosen or how sensitive results are to this choice.
- The distinction between the radiance MLP U and occupancy MLP G could be better motivated in terms of the scene properties they model.

The paper would benefit from a more pedagogical presentation that walks readers through the neural point field construction step-by-step with clearer notation and geometric intuition.

#### Tracking Loss Design (Equation 10)

The justification for excluding color information from the tracking loss—"Considering the strong non-convexity of the color map, only the geometry part is included in tracking loss"—is asserted rather than demonstrated. This design choice merits empirical validation through ablation studies. The

reviews.original_rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: There have been some papers on getting dense Visual SLAM with neural fields published in the last couple of years. In this work, the authors proposed a set of additions to prior pipelines that get the NN SLAM closer to the traditional approaches concerning the overall framework. Specifically, the authors addressed loop detection, pose graph optimization, bundle adjustment, and collaborative SLAM.
reviews.strengths:
1) Visual-SLAM with neural fields has the huge benefit of providing a dense map of the environment. The topic is relevant to computer vision, robotics, and I believe it fits the NeurIPS scope.
2) The paper is well-written, easy to follow, and well-organized. Related work is OK.
3) Although the technical novelties are relatively limited, in my opinion, the contributions to the pipeline are significant.
reviews.weaknesses:
1) As written above, the technical contributions are limited. The paper focuses more on adding new steps to prior Visual SLAM pipelines.
2) The inclusion of the NetVLAD (not new) as a tool for loop detection looks like a trick. It is not elegant, in the sense that it is a parallel feature extractor that (maybe) could be done using the neural field encoders.
3) Only one dataset and 4 sequences are used, which is small concerning quantity and variety.
4) I would prefer to see more comparisons with previous traditional methods (without neural fields).
5) Figures 5 and 6 are not easily readable.
reviews.questions:
1) I would like the authors to explain what is the motivation for the inclusion of NetVLAD. Can't those descriptors be obtained from the neural field?
2) It is written that a pre-trained NetVLAD was used. It would be important to give more details about this training and how it generalizes to different scenes.
3) Usually, with classical V-SLAM, the bundle adjustment estimates both the pose of the cameras and the 3D map simultaneously. It seems that the authors are doing this alternatively. I would like to see some comments on this.
4) Why are the results for Apartment 2 so bad for the proposed approach? I would like the authors to comment on this.
5) It seems that traditional V-SLAM methods using RGB-D are missing (such as OrbSLAM). I would like to see some comments on this.
6) Maybe I missed something. But it is not clear to me how is the error in Tab. 2 computed.
reviews.limitations: Limitations are adequately addressed.
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
There have been some papers on getting dense Visual SLAM with neural fields published in the last couple of years. In this work, the authors proposed a set of additions to prior pipelines that get the NN SLAM closer to the traditional approaches concerning the overall framework. Specifically, the authors addressed loop detection, pose graph optimization, bundle adjustment, and collaborative SLAM.

Strengths:
1) Visual-SLAM with neural fields has the huge benefit of providing a dense map of the environment. The topic is relevant to computer vision, robotics, and I believe it fits the NeurIPS scope.
2) The paper is well-written, easy to follow, and well-organized. Related work is OK.
3) Although the technical novelties are relatively limited, in my opinion, the contributions to the pipeline are significant.

Weaknesses:
1) As written above, the technical contributions are limited. The paper focuses more on adding new steps to prior Visual SLAM pipelines.
2) The inclusion of the NetVLAD (not new) as a tool for loop detection looks like a trick. It is not elegant, in the sense that it is a parallel feature extractor that (maybe) could be done using the neural field encoders.
3) Only one dataset and 4 sequences are used, which is small concerning quantity and variety.
4) I would prefer to see more comparisons with previous traditional methods (without neural fields).
5) Figures 5 and 6 are not easily readable.

Questions:
1) I would like the authors to explain what is the motivation for the inclusion of NetVLAD. Can't those descriptors be obtained from the neural field?
2) It is written that a pre-trained NetVLAD was used. It would be important to give more details about this training and how it generalizes to different scenes.
3) Usually, with classical V-SLAM, the bundle adjustment estimates both the pose of the cameras and the 3D map simultaneously. It seems that the authors are doing this alternatively. I would like to see some comments on this.
4) Why are the results for Apartment 2 so bad for the proposed approach? I would like the authors to comment on this.
5) It seems that traditional V-SLAM methods using RGB-D are missing (such as OrbSLAM). I would like to see some comments on this.
6) Maybe I missed something. But it is not clear to me how is the error in Tab. 2 computed.

Limitations:
Limitations are adequately addressed.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review: CP-SLAM: Collaborative Neural Point-based SLAM

## Summary

This paper presents CP-SLAM, a collaborative neural point-based SLAM system that extends recent neural field-based visual SLAM approaches by incorporating classical SLAM pipeline components. The authors address several limitations of prior neural implicit SLAM methods (such as NICE-SLAM and Vox-Fusion) by introducing loop detection, pose graph optimization, sub-map fusion, and support for multi-agent collaborative mapping. The work aims to bridge the gap between learning-based dense reconstruction methods and the complete, robust frameworks that traditional SLAM systems provide. While the topic is timely and the system design is comprehensive, the technical novelties are primarily architectural rather than methodologically novel.

## Strengths

1) **Relevant and Timely Topic**: Visual SLAM with neural fields represents an important frontier in computer vision, offering significant advantages in dense map reconstruction compared to sparse feature-based approaches. The problem is highly relevant to robotics, autonomous systems, augmented reality, and computer vision broadly. The paper's focus on extending neural SLAM to multi-agent scenarios addresses a practical need that has received limited attention in the literature. The work fits well within the NeurIPS scope, bridging deep learning and robotics.

2) **Well-Organized Presentation and Clarity**: The paper is generally well-written and easy to follow. The system overview in Figure 1 effectively communicates the pipeline flow, and the problem motivation is clearly articulated. The authors properly contextualize their work relative to both traditional SLAM systems and recent neural implicit approaches. The organization of the method section, progressing from odometry through loop detection to global optimization, follows a logical structure that makes the overall contribution digestible.

3) **Comprehensive System Design**: Although individual technical components may not be novel, the integration of these components into a cohesive system represents a meaningful contribution. The paper successfully demonstrates how classical SLAM concepts—loop closure, pose graph optimization, and bundle adjustment—can be adapted to work with neural implicit representations. The keyframe-centric neural point representation is a clever design choice that enables these classical operations. This systematic approach to building a complete SLAM pipeline with neural fields is valuable for the community, as it shows how to combine the benefits of both paradigms.

4) **Strong Experimental Results**: The quantitative results demonstrate consistent improvements over recent neural SLAM methods (NICE-SLAM, Vox-Fusion) and competitive or superior performance compared to traditional methods like ORB-SLAM3. The improvements in both tracking accuracy (Table 2) and reconstruction quality (Table 4) are substantial and consistent across multiple sequences. The ablation studies (Section 4.4) effectively validate the importance of key components, particularly the pose graph optimization module which yields ~10-13% improvements.

## Weaknesses

1) **Limited Technical Novelty**: As the authors acknowledge implicitly and the reviewer notes explicitly, the core technical contributions are limited. The paper primarily combines existing techniques—neural radiance field rendering from NeRF, point-based representations inspired by Point-NeRF, NetVLAD for loop detection, and standard pose graph optimization from traditional SLAM. While integration is valuable, each individual component is borrowed from prior work. The neural point representation, while effective, is a relatively straightforward adaptation of Point-NeRF. The distributed-to-centralized learning strategy using federated averaging is a reasonable but not particularly novel approach to multi-agent learning. The paper would be strengthened by more fundamental algorithmic innovations that are specifically tailored to the neural SLAM setting.

2) **Inelegant Use of NetVLAD for Loop Detection**: The reliance on NetVLAD as an external pre-trained model for loop detection feels like a workaround rather than an integrated solution. The authors use a parallel feature extractor (NetVLAD) that operates independently of the neural field encoders, which seems suboptimal and raises important questions about design choices. The paper does not adequately justify why loop detection descriptors cannot be derived directly from the learned neural field representations or from the MLP encoders used for scene reconstruction. A more elegant approach would leverage the neural field's learned features for this critical task, reducing dependency on external models and creating a more unified system. The authors should provide clearer motivation for this design decision and discuss potential alternatives. Additionally, the paper mentions that a pre-trained NetVLAD model is used, but lacks sufficient detail about how this pre-trained model generalizes across the diverse synthetic scenes tested. Does the model require fine-tuning for different environments? How does performance degrade in scenarios significantly different from NetVLAD's training distribution?

3) **Limited Dataset Evaluation and Lack of Variety**: A significant limitation is the reliance on only the Replica dataset with just 4 sequences for single-agent experiments and 4 sequences for two-agent experiments. This is relatively small in terms of both quantity and diversity. The Replica dataset, while high-quality, consists of synthetic indoor scenes with consistent lighting and texture characteristics. The paper would benefit substantially from evaluation on additional datasets with greater variety in terms of scene complexity, lighting conditions, texture characteristics, and camera motion patterns. While the authors include real-world experiments on TUM-RGBD (Table 3), these are limited to 3 sequences and show only marginal improvements over baselines. The lack of diverse evaluation makes it difficult to assess the robustness and generalization capability of the proposed approach. Furthermore, the synthetic nature of the primary evaluation dataset raises questions about how well the method would perform in challenging real-world scenarios with significant appearance variations, motion blur, or dynamic objects.

4) **Insufficient Comparison with Traditional RGB-D SLAM Methods**: The paper includes comparisons with ORB-SLAM3, which is presented as a traditional baseline. However, ORB-SLAM3 is not specifically designed for dense RGB-D SLAM and requires concatenation of sequences to process multiple streams, which is not its intended use case. The paper would benefit from comparisons with other traditional dense RGB-D SLAM methods that are more directly comparable. Methods like ElasticFusion, BundleFusion, or other dense RGB-D approaches would provide more appropriate baselines for evaluating the dense mapping capabilities. The current comparison setup somewhat unfairly positions ORB-SLAM3, as it is being evaluated outside its design scope. Additionally, more extensive comparisons with traditional methods would help contextualize the benefits of the neural approach versus classical geometry-based methods.

5) **Poor Visualization Quality in Key Figures**: Figures 5 and 6, which present trajectory comparisons and qualitative results, are difficult to read and interpret. The trajectories in these figures are small, lack sufficient contrast, and the legend information is hard to distinguish. For Figure 5, the trajectory plots would benefit from larger visualizations, different colors or line styles for better distinction, and perhaps zoomed-in views of critical regions where differences are most apparent. Similarly, Figure 6's trajectory comparisons would be more informative with clearer visual separation between methods. These figures are important for conveying the qualitative performance differences and deserve higher presentation quality. The authors should consider using larger subplots, improved color schemes, and perhaps adding quantitative annotations directly to the figures.

6) **Incomplete Analysis of Failure Cases and Limitations**: While Section 5 mentions some limitations (GPU requirements, hole-filling ability, large viewpoint changes), the discussion is somewhat superficial. The paper would benefit from more detailed analysis of when and why the method fails. For instance, why do results for Apartment-2 (Table 1) show notably worse performance compared to other scenes? This particular failure case deserves investigation and explanation. Are there specific scene characteristics that challenge the method? The authors should provide more concrete examples and analysis of failure modes, which would help readers understand the method's applicability boundaries and guide future improvements.

## Questions and Clarifications

1) **NetVLAD Integration and Design Motivation**: The authors use a pre-trained NetVLAD model as a separate feature extractor for loop detection. Could the authors explain in detail why descriptors for loop closure cannot be obtained directly from the neural field's learned representations? Have the authors experimented with extracting loop closure features from the MLP encoders or the neural point features themselves? This seems like it would create a more unified and elegant system. Additionally, what is the computational overhead of running NetVLAD in parallel with the main SLAM pipeline?

2) **NetVLAD Generalization Across Scenes**: The paper states that a pre-trained NetVLAD model is used, but provides limited details about this model's training and generalization properties. Was the model trained on data similar to Replica? How does it perform on scenes with significantly different visual characteristics? Does the model require scene-specific fine-tuning, or does it generalize well out-of-the-box? These details are important for understanding the practical applicability of the approach.

3) **Bundle Adjustment Strategy**: In classical visual SLAM, bundle adjustment typically optimizes both camera poses and 3D map structure simultaneously in a joint optimization framework. The paper appears to perform these optimizations sequentially or separately—first optimizing the neural field during mapping, then optimizing poses during pose graph optimization, followed by map refinement. Could the authors clarify the optimization strategy and discuss why
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
There have been some papers on getting dense Visual SLAM with neural fields published in the last couple of years. In this work, the authors proposed a set of additions to prior pipelines that get the NN SLAM closer to the traditional approaches concerning the overall framework. Specifically, the authors addressed loop detection, pose graph optimization, bundle adjustment, and collaborative SLAM.

Strengths:
1) Visual-SLAM with neural fields has the huge benefit of providing a dense map of the environment. The topic is relevant to computer vision, robotics, and I believe it fits the NeurIPS scope.
2) The paper is well-written, easy to follow, and well-organized. Related work is OK.
3) Although the technical novelties are relatively limited, in my opinion, the contributions to the pipeline are significant.

Weaknesses:
1) As written above, the technical contributions are limited. The paper focuses more on adding new steps to prior Visual SLAM pipelines.
2) The inclusion of the NetVLAD (not new) as a tool for loop detection looks like a trick. It is not elegant, in the sense that it is a parallel feature extractor that (maybe) could be done using the neural field encoders.
3) Only one dataset and 4 sequences are used, which is small concerning quantity and variety.
4) I would prefer to see more comparisons with previous traditional methods (without neural fields).
5) Figures 5 and 6 are not easily readable.

Questions:
1) I would like the authors to explain what is the motivation for the inclusion of NetVLAD. Can't those descriptors be obtained from the neural field?
2) It is written that a pre-trained NetVLAD was used. It would be important to give more details about this training and how it generalizes to different scenes.
3) Usually, with classical V-SLAM, the bundle adjustment estimates both the pose of the cameras and the 3D map simultaneously. It seems that the authors are doing this alternatively. I would like to see some comments on this.
4) Why are the results for Apartment 2 so bad for the proposed approach? I would like the authors to comment on this.
5) It seems that traditional V-SLAM methods using RGB-D are missing (such as OrbSLAM). I would like to see some comments on this.
6) Maybe I missed something. But it is not clear to me how is the error in Tab. 2 computed.

Limitations:
Limitations are adequately addressed.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary

Starting with the observation that supervised embeddings should vary from one class to another but not be sensitive to variations within a given class, this paper proposes to add an intra-class correlation (ICC) regularization to the contrastive loss in representation learning. This new regularizer is derived from measurement theory and encourages the embeddings to have a low intra-class variance, whereas contrastive losses push more towards high inter-class variances. After a simulation illustrating the "behaviour" of these losses with respect to inter- and intra-class variance, the authors report good improvements using the proposed ICC regularizer for three different tasks spanning speaker verification, voice style conversion, and clinical dysphonia detection.

## Strengths

### Clarity and Presentation

- The paper is overall well written, the idea well explained and easy to follow and to understand. The authors effectively bridge the concept of repeatability from measurement theory to the machine learning domain, making the motivation clear and compelling. The connection between measurement repeatability and embedding quality is intuitive and well-motivated through concrete examples such as speaker verification across varying utterance content and clinical voice assessments across multiple days.

- The ICC regularizer seems to really focus on intra-class variance, i.e., make all occurrences of the same class have the same embeddings, where contrastive loss does both at the same time and never really focuses on minimizing the intra-class variance. This distinction is particularly important because it addresses a specific limitation of existing approaches: while contrastive losses like GE2E simultaneously optimize both intra-class and inter-class variance, they do not explicitly prioritize minimizing within-class variation when the between-class separation is already sufficient.

- The method is relatively simple, very well explained and easy to understand. The mathematical formulation in Equations 1-6 is straightforward, and the authors provide clear interpretations of each component. The regularizer is implemented as a simple additive term (Equation 6: R_ICC = 1 - ICC(e)), making it practical to integrate into existing training pipelines.

### Theoretical Analysis and Intuition

- The simulation, comparison with contrastive loss and classification error rate is quite interesting and provides valuable insights. Figure 1 effectively visualizes the fundamental differences between the two approaches. The Monte Carlo simulation with varying intra- and inter-class variance pairs (from 0.02 to 2.0 for intra-class and 0.01 to 0.60 for inter-class) demonstrates that the ICC regularizer and GE2E loss have distinctly different optimization landscapes. The gradient analysis in Equations 7-8 provides mathematical justification for why the ICC regularizer places greater emphasis on minimizing intra-class variance once inter-class separation is adequate.

- When the embeddings are constrained, it is indeed useful to focus on minimizing the intra-class variance to separate classes. The explanation and results are convincing. The authors make a compelling argument that L2 normalization (Equation 1) places an upper bound on total variance, limiting how large inter-class variance can become. Under these constraints, a regularizer that prioritizes intra-class variance minimization naturally leads to better repeatability than one attempting to simultaneously maximize inter-class variance. The observation that GE2E's gradient descent continues to increase inter-class variance even when intra-class variance is already small (point 1 in Figure 1(a)) effectively illustrates this inefficiency.

- The connection between ICC regularizer values and SVM classification error rates (Figure 1(c)) provides additional evidence that the proposed regularizer aligns well with downstream task performance. The similar contour patterns between the ICC regularizer and classification error suggest that optimizing for repeatability via the ICC metric has practical benefits beyond just the metric itself.

### Comprehensive Experimental Validation

- The paper demonstrates the effectiveness of the ICC regularizer across three diverse speech applications, each with distinct characteristics and requirements. This breadth of evaluation strengthens the claim that the approach has general utility beyond a single domain.

- In Task 1 (text-independent speaker verification), the experimental setup is rigorous, testing multiple architectures (VGG-M-40 and FastResNet-34) and multiple baseline contrastive losses (GE2E, AngleProto, SupCon). The consistent improvements across all baselines—approximately 10% EER reduction for VGG-M-40 with GE2E and 3.3-6.08% improvements with SupCon—demonstrate the method's robustness. The ICC scores also improve consistently (from 0.4494 to 0.5487 for VGG-M-40 with GE2E), directly validating that the regularizer achieves its stated objective of improving embedding repeatability.

- Task 2 (zero-shot voice style conversion) provides an interesting downstream application test. The AB preference test with 18 listeners and 100 speaker pairs provides meaningful human evaluation, with 61.90% preference for embeddings trained with the ICC regularizer. The objective metrics (speaker similarity, WER, CER) all improve, providing quantitative confirmation of the qualitative preference results. This task is particularly valuable because it demonstrates that improved repeatability translates to better performance in an application where embeddings serve as style guides rather than classification features.

- Task 3 (dysphonic voice detection) addresses a clinical application where repeatability is especially important for medical reliability. The 30.68% improvement in ICC score (from 0.4368 to 0.5708) is substantial and clinically meaningful. The cross-corpus generalization results (MEEI: 0.8214, HUPA: 0.6894) demonstrate that more repeatable embeddings improve not just in-corpus performance but also the model's ability to generalize to different recording conditions and speaker populations, which is critical for clinical deployment.

- The authors provide extensive implementation details in the appendices, including handling of imbalanced classes (Appendix A), hyperparameter ablation studies (Appendix B), and detailed model architectures for the dysphonia task (Appendix C). The discussion of convergence issues and the evolution from one-embedding to two-branch structures (Section C.1) provides valuable insights into practical challenges and solutions.

### Reproducibility and Open Science

- The authors commit to reproducibility by providing open-source code in their GitHub repository, which is essential for allowing the community to reproduce results and build upon this work. The detailed experimental protocols, hyperparameter specifications, and dataset descriptions in the appendices further support reproducibility.

## Weaknesses

### Limited Comparative Analysis with Related Methods

- Missing comparison with other techniques mentioned in the related work (data augmentation, etc.). Is the proposed approach competitive with these methods? Could it be complementary? The paper mentions several alternative approaches to handling intra-class variation, including data augmentation techniques (combining datasets from different domains, data warping, GAN-based augmentation) and novel learning paradigms (variance-invariance-covariance regularization, invariant risk minimization). However, no experimental comparisons are provided against these methods. It would be valuable to understand whether the ICC regularizer is superior to, complementary with, or orthogonal to these existing approaches. For instance, could the ICC regularizer be combined with data augmentation for even better results?

- How does it compare to VICReg [4] for example? VICReg (variance-invariance-covariance regularization) is specifically designed to handle variance and invariance properties in self-supervised learning and would seem like a natural baseline for comparison, particularly given its explicit focus on variance control. The paper cites VICReg in the related work but does not empirically compare against it. A direct comparison would help position the ICC regularizer within the landscape of variance-focused regularization methods.

- The paper would benefit from ablation studies examining the interaction between the ICC regularizer and other variance-reduction techniques. For example, how does adding the ICC regularizer to models that already employ data augmentation strategies affect performance? Are there diminishing returns, or is the ICC regularizer orthogonal to these approaches?

### Presentation of Results and Analysis Depth

- In the result section, the text mainly lists the results in the tables. Maybe a reformulation would allow more space for example to study the effect of the regularization weight. While Tables 1, 2, and 3 present the numerical results clearly, the accompanying text in Sections 4.1-4.3 primarily summarizes these numbers without deeper analysis. For instance, the paper does not discuss how performance scales with the ICC regularizer weight (λ) across different tasks. The hyperparameter values differ significantly between tasks (λ = 0.06 for VGG-M-40, λ = 0.25 for FastResNet-34, λ = 0.5 for dysphonia detection), but there is limited discussion of why these differences exist or how sensitive the method is to these choices.

- While Appendix B provides contour plots for different λ values (Figure B.1), the main paper could benefit from more discussion of the hyperparameter tuning process. How was the grid search conducted? What was the range of λ values tested? Were there consistent patterns

reviews.text:
# Expanded Review: Learning Repeatable Speech Embeddings Using An Intra-class Correlation Regularizer

## Summary

The paper presents a straightforward yet seemingly effective approach to regularizing contrastive embedding extractors through the introduction of an intra-class correlation (ICC) regularizer. The core contribution lies in leveraging the ICC metric from measurement theory to ensure the repeatability of learned embeddings—a property that characterizes consistency when the underlying conditions remain unaltered. The authors propose using the ICC both as an evaluation metric for embedding quality and as a complementary regularization component to traditional contrastive losses. The effectiveness of this approach is demonstrated empirically across three distinct speech-related tasks: text-independent speaker verification, zero-shot voice style conversion, and a clinical application for dysphonic voice detection. The experimental validation appears comprehensive and the results are encouraging across all three domains.

## Strengths

**1. Well-motivated and clearly presented core idea:** The paper successfully bridges concepts from measurement theory (repeatability) with modern deep learning representation learning. The connection between repeatability and embedding quality is well-articulated and intuitive. The authors effectively motivate why repeatability matters, particularly in speech applications where numerous confounding factors (speaker neurological state, recording setup, environmental conditions) can introduce unwanted variability. This conceptual contribution provides a fresh perspective on embedding learning.

**2. Insightful theoretical analysis:** The comparison between the ICC regularizer and the GE2E contrastive loss in Section 3.2 is particularly valuable. The Monte Carlo simulation-based analysis effectively illustrates the fundamental differences in optimization objectives between the two approaches. The contour plots in Figure 1 provide clear visual evidence that the ICC regularizer focuses more aggressively on minimizing intra-class variance while placing less emphasis on further increasing inter-class variance once adequate separation is achieved. The gradient analysis provided in Equations 7 and 8 offers mathematical support for these observations, explaining why the ICC regularizer exhibits different optimization trajectories than the GE2E loss. This theoretical grounding elevates the contribution beyond a purely empirical observation.

**3. Comprehensive experimental validation:** The evaluation spans three diverse application domains, demonstrating the generalizability of the approach. For text-independent speaker verification, the paper tests multiple contrastive loss baselines (GE2E, AngleProto, SupCon) across two different network architectures (VGG-M-40 and FastResNet-34), showing consistent improvements. The ~10% EER reduction for VGG-M-40 with GE2E is substantial. The zero-shot voice style conversion task includes both subjective evaluation (AB preference test with 18 listeners showing 61.90% preference for more repeatable embeddings) and objective metrics (speaker similarity, WER, CER), providing multi-faceted assessment. The dysphonic voice detection task demonstrates clinical relevance and includes cross-corpus generalization testing, which is particularly important for medical applications. The improvement in ICC score from 0.4368 to 0.5708 (30.68% improvement) for the dysphonic detection task is noteworthy.

**4. Reproducibility and transparency:** The authors provide open-source code on GitHub and include detailed experimental setup descriptions, including hyperparameter values, batch compositions, and training procedures. This commitment to reproducibility is commendable and facilitates future research building upon this work. The detailed appendices covering imbalanced class handling, hyperparameter ablation studies, and model architecture justifications demonstrate thoroughness.

**5. Practical applicability:** The method is straightforward to implement and can be readily integrated into existing training pipelines as an additional regularization term. The linear combination formulation (Lcontr + λRICC) is simple and familiar to practitioners, lowering the barrier to adoption.

## Weaknesses

**1. Limited to second-order statistics:** The ICC regularizer fundamentally relies on second-order statistics (mean and variance) for each embedding dimension independently. This represents a significant limitation that deserves more extensive discussion. While the authors note this in the limitations section, the implications warrant deeper exploration. The use of only mean and variance may not capture higher-order distributional properties or complex geometric structures in the embedding space. For instance, embeddings with identical first and second moments but different higher-order characteristics (skewness, kurtosis) would be treated identically by the ICC regularizer. This could potentially limit the regularizer's ability to enforce more sophisticated forms of consistency or to detect and correct for multi-modal intra-class distributions. The paper would benefit from empirical investigation of whether higher-order statistics might improve performance, or conversely, evidence that second-order statistics are sufficient for the target applications.

**2. Insufficient analysis of the relationship between ICC and mutual information:** The reviewer raises an important theoretical question that deserves more thorough treatment. Contrastive losses have well-established connections to mutual information maximization, particularly when viewed through the lens of information theory. The paper does not adequately explore whether the ICC-based approach captures information-theoretic properties that contrastive losses might miss, or conversely, whether the ICC is implicitly captured by contrastive losses when only second-order statistics are considered. The authors claim that contrastive losses optimize both intra-class and inter-class variance simultaneously, while the ICC regularizer emphasizes intra-class variance reduction, but this explanation does not fully address the deeper theoretical question of how ICC relates to information-theoretic objectives. A more rigorous analysis of the information-theoretic foundations would strengthen the contribution. For instance, does the ICC regularizer implicitly maximize mutual information between embeddings of the same class? How does this relate to established bounds on mutual information estimation from finite samples?

**3. Dimension-independent computation concerns:** The ICC is computed for each embedding dimension independently (as shown in Equations 3-5), and then the mean ICC score is used as the overall metric. The authors acknowledge in Section 3.2 that "high separability in each embedding dimension doesn't necessarily translate into good separability in the overall embedding space and can lead to learned embedding dimensions that are highly correlated." However, this critical observation is somewhat buried and not thoroughly investigated. The paper would benefit from: (1) empirical analysis of correlation between embedding dimensions when the ICC regularizer is used, (2) comparison of embedding dimension correlations with and without the regularizer, and (3) investigation of whether the regularizer inadvertently encourages dimensional redundancy. While the authors argue this is why the ICC should be used as a regularizer rather than a standalone loss, more evidence supporting this design choice would be valuable.

**4. Hyperparameter sensitivity and tuning requirements:** The paper requires careful tuning of the hyperparameter λ when combining the contrastive loss with the ICC regularizer. The values used vary significantly across experiments (0.06 for VGG-M-40 with GE2E, 0.25 for FastResNet-34 with GE2E, 0.5 for dysphonic detection, etc.), suggesting that λ is highly task and architecture dependent. While Appendix B provides contour plots showing how different λ values affect the combined loss landscape, the paper lacks principled guidelines for selecting λ without extensive grid search. The authors acknowledge this requirement but do not provide strategies for practitioners to efficiently determine appropriate λ values for new tasks. Sensitivity analysis showing how performance varies across a range of λ values for each task would be helpful. Additionally, the different λ values across tasks suggest that the regularizer's importance varies depending on the application, but this variation is not adequately explained or analyzed.

**5. Limited theoretical justification for the ICC choice:** While the ICC is well-established in measurement theory, the paper could provide stronger theoretical justification for why the ICC(1,1) formulation specifically is the appropriate choice for embedding regularization. The authors note that ICC(1,1) measures "absolute agreement," but do not thoroughly discuss alternative ICC formulations (e.g., ICC(2,1), ICC(3,1)) or justify why absolute agreement is the most suitable criterion. Furthermore, the connection between ICC and embedding quality could be made more explicit. Why is ICC the optimal metric for repeatability in the context of deep learning embeddings? Are there alternative metrics from measurement theory that might be more suitable? The paper would benefit from ablation studies comparing different ICC formulations or alternative repeatability metrics.

**6. Limited analysis of failure cases or negative results:** The paper does not discuss scenarios where the ICC regularizer might be less effective or potentially harmful. For instance, are there tasks or domains where enforcing high repeatability could be counterproductive? The dysphonic voice detection task shows interesting results where the proposed method achieves lower training accuracy (0.7353) compared to the Zhang et al. baseline (0.8003) on the SVD training set, yet achieves better cross-corpus generalization. This trade-off deserves more discussion. What is the mechanism by which lower in-corpus accuracy translates to better generalization? Does the ICC regularizer act as an implicit regularizer against overfitting? More analysis of such trade-offs would provide valuable insights.

## Questions and Concerns

**1. Information-theoretic foundations:** The relationship between the ICC regularizer and mutual information maximization deserves clarification. Given that contrastive losses have direct connections to mutual information, what is the theoretical relationship
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper presents a simple but seemingly effective idea to regularize contrastive embedding extractors. The regularizer aims at the intra-class correlation to ensure the repeatability of the embedding.
The effectiveness is confirmed experimentally for the task of speaker verification, voice style conversion, and dysphonic voice detection.

Strengths:
The presented idea is well introduced and the illustration of the ICC regularizer action compared to the GE2E loss is insightful. The evaluation is sufficient.

Weaknesses:
The introduced regularizer uses 2nd order statistics only which could be a limiting factor on the embedding extractors but on the other hand this doesn't need to be required.

Questions:
The contrastive loss has a direct relationship to the mutual information, one could think that ICC is automatically included in the mutual information when only 2nd order statistics are taken into account, what could be the reason that this doesn't seem to be the case.

Limitations:
The introduced regularizer uses 2nd order statistics only.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

In this paper, authors propose to use an intra-class correlation coefficient (ICC) regularizer as a complimentary component for contrastive loss to increase the repeatability of the supervised speech embedding. Authors conduct simulation for ICC regularizer and contrastive loss (GE2E loss) to explain their similarities and differences in the optimization criteria. Based on the experimental results on the speaker verification, voice style conversion, and a clinical application for detecting dysphonic three tasks, the authors claim that adding an ICC regularizer helps increase the repeatability and further improve the performance on downstream tasks.

Strengths:
1. This paper leverages the concept of repeatability and states that higher repeatability can contribute to improved performance in downstream tasks. Authors think about the importance of intra-class variance and the proposed ICC regularizer places greater emphasis on minimizing it to help increase repeatability. The simulation results in Section 3.2 explains the intuition clearly. 
 
2. As a complementary component to contrastive loss, adding the proposed ICC regularizer successfully increase the repeatability and shows good performance in the three different tasks.

Weaknesses:
1. In Task 1, the performance of SV system is not good though adding the ICC regularizer improves its performance. In this paper, VGG-M-40 is trained on VoxCeleb1 & 2. The best result (3.78% EER) reported in Table 1 is not as good as other commonly used models for SV. In Chung et al. paper, the VGG-M-40 shows much worse performance than Thin-ResNet34 and Fast ResNet34. To make the experimental solid and results and claims more convincible, authors should pick a good baseline instead of use the VGG-M-40.     

2. For better replicable, more experiment details should be provided, such as the training details of Task 1.     

3. To have comprehensive evaluation results for Task 1, the evaluation metric minDCF can be used as well.

4. Visualization (such as t-SNE) can be added as a comparison to see if the extracted speech or speaker embeddings have smaller intra-class variance in the space, though their ICC is decreased.

Questions:
1. As this paper mentioned, the ICC regularizer requires hyperparameter fine-tuning. Are the hyperparameter tuned on a validation set or a development set? 

2. As this paper mentioned in Section 3.2, though the ICC increased, do the learned embeddings are more compacted within-class and more separate between-class in the overall embedding space? Authors can use t-SNE to visualize them.

Limitations:
The authors do not analyze the potential negative societal impact.
