Summary:
This paper proposes two new metrics allowing to measure and explain the diversity of a generated set of images w.r.t a training set. Instead of the usual distributional distances relying upon an embedding space from a pre-trained model, these metrics rely on a set of textual attributes. The similarity between an image and an attribute is computed using their representation in a common semantic space, via the CLIP model - vectors are shifted using a centre of training images/attributes to make similarity scores more meaningful. Several ways to obtain attributes (Captioning, User-based or GPT-based) are investigated. The usefulness of the metrics is tested with an experiment injecting images correlated with target attributes in data, an experiment aiming to detect a specific attribute relationship in a curated dataset, and in a comparison of several generative models.

Strengths:
- This paper proposes an interesting approach, aiming at using the common semantic space between images and text proposed by CLIP to measure attribute and image relatedness, in order to provide interpretable representations of generated images and measure attribute-based metrics between a reference and generated dataset; such an application seems relatively original to me. 
- The two first experiments demonstrate well the usefulness of the approach, able to detect a shift when the data has been curated by human, based on which attributes.

Weaknesses:
- The presentation of the paper could be improved upon. This include the writing, and the readability of the figures, as well as the quantity of information provided. This last points concerns mainly the presentation and framing of the problem of interpretability of representations, as well as the presentation and motivation of the experimental settings. 
- In particular, the paper lacks related work on concept-based representation for interpretability of images. While building metrics dedicated to generative model seems new to me, there are based on an idea which has been explored extensively before. See for example "Concept Whitening for Interpretable Image Recognition, Chen et al, 2020". 
- The paper focuses on a narrow choice of methods to generate attributes, which, to me, should be one of the key experimental investigation of the paper. Notably, the previous literature explores using different kind of attributes, coming from existing data (for example, "Interpretable Basis Decomposition for Visual Explanation, Zhou et al, 2018") or to be learnt ("A Framework to Learn with Interpretation", Parekh et al, 2021). The authors only (very shortly) argue about the number of needed attributes.
- The toy experiments seem relevant but are very fastly presented and should be expanded upon. The remaining experiments are too short to be convincing and only focus on a handful of models.

Questions:
- Could you better formalize the notation $DCS_{\mathcal{X}}(a_{i,j})$ for joint distributions ?
- Is it necessary to center the text and images differently ? They lie in the same semantic space. Also, would it not be reasonable to be using the attributes themselves, rather than captions ?

Limitations:
- Previous distributional metrics are several times referred as relying on external models in your paper. However, the attributes that you use also rely on external models (except the USER one, of course - but in this case, the computing of DCS still relies on a captioning model). How would you address this issue ?

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Expanded Review: Attribute Based Interpretable Evaluation Metrics for Generative Models

## Summary

This paper proposes two novel metrics—Single attribute KL Divergence (SaKLD) and Paired attribute KL Divergence (PaKLD)—for measuring and explaining the diversity of generated image sets relative to training sets. Rather than relying on distributional distances computed in embedding spaces from pre-trained classification models, these metrics operate on a set of textual attributes that characterize images. The similarity between an image and an attribute is computed using their joint representation in the semantic space provided by CLIP, with vectors strategically shifted using the center of training images and attribute embeddings to produce more meaningful and interpretable similarity scores. The paper investigates multiple approaches for obtaining attributes (automatic extraction via image captioning with BLIP, user annotation via CelebA attributes, and generation via GPT-3 prompting), and validates the usefulness of the proposed metrics through targeted experiments including correlated image injection, attribute relationship detection, and comparison across several state-of-the-art generative models including StyleGAN variants and diffusion models.

## Strengths

- **Novel application of semantic spaces for interpretability**: This paper proposes an interesting and relatively original approach to leveraging CLIP's common semantic space between images and text to measure attribute-image relatedness. The application of this idea to provide interpretable representations of generated images and to compute attribute-based metrics between reference and generated datasets appears novel in the context of generative model evaluation. The core insight—that distributional metrics can be made interpretable by grounding them in semantic attributes rather than opaque embedding dimensions—is compelling and well-motivated.

- **Directional CLIPScore as an improvement over raw similarity**: The introduction of Directional CLIPScore (DCS) addresses a real limitation of naive CLIPScore computation. By shifting vectors to use the center of training images and attribute embeddings as reference points (Equation 3-4), the authors effectively resolve the problem of attribute similarities clustering too closely together. The empirical validation in Table 1 demonstrates that DCS achieves higher accuracy (0.530 vs 0.409 mean accuracy on refined CelebA attributes) compared to raw CLIPScore, providing concrete evidence that this methodological refinement produces more discriminative scores.

- **Well-designed validation experiments**: The first two experiments (correlated image injection in Section 5.1 and attribute relationship detection in Section 5.2) effectively demonstrate the utility and superiority of the proposed approach. The correlated image injection experiment is particularly well-conceived, showing that SaKLD and PaKLD detect attribute distribution shifts more sensitively than FID (Table 2). The toy experiment in Section 5.2 elegantly demonstrates a scenario where SaKLD fails but PaKLD succeeds, making a clear case for the necessity of considering joint attribute distributions. Figure 4 effectively illustrates how PaKLD can identify specific problematic attribute pairs like ("woman", "smiling") that SaKLD misses.

- **Interesting findings regarding model performance differences**: The comparison of generative models across datasets (Tables 3-4) reveals non-obvious patterns, such as StyleGAN2-ADA's surprisingly poor performance and diffusion models' weakness on color attributes. The subsequent analysis in Table 4 and the discussion referencing Khrulkov et al.'s work on optimal transport maps provides valuable insight into why diffusion models struggle with color preservation, suggesting that the proposed metrics can reveal model-specific weaknesses that traditional metrics like FID obscure.

## Weaknesses

### Presentation and Clarity Issues

- **Insufficient motivation for interpretability problem**: While the paper claims to address the lack of interpretability in existing metrics, the framing of this problem could be substantially strengthened. The introduction motivates interpretability through the example in Figure 1, where humans can identify that "Model 2 is biased with 'long hair' and 'makeup'." However, the paper does not adequately discuss the broader context of concept-based interpretability in computer vision, which would help readers understand why this problem matters and how it connects to existing work in explainable AI. The paper would benefit from a more thorough discussion of what "interpretability" means in this context and why it is important beyond simply providing post-hoc explanations.

- **Presentation of experimental settings lacks clarity**: The experimental sections, particularly Sections 5.1 and 5.2, present their setups and motivations too briefly. For instance, in Section 5.1, the choice to inject 300 or 600 edited images into a 30,000-image subset is not well justified. Why these specific numbers? How sensitive are the results to these choices? Similarly, the curated dataset construction in Section 5.2 (20,000 smiling men and 20,000 non-smiling women) is presented as a "toy experiment" without sufficient discussion of why this particular configuration was chosen or how it generalizes to real-world model failures. Expanding these sections to include more detailed motivation and sensitivity analysis would strengthen the paper considerably.

- **Figure quality and readability**: Several figures could be improved for clarity. Figure 2, which is meant to illustrate the difference between CLIPScore and DCS, uses small text and could benefit from larger, more legible font sizes and clearer visual separation of the concepts. Figure 3's illustration of the vector geometry would be clearer with explicit angle markings and more detailed captions explaining the geometric interpretation. The notation in Figure 3 could also be better integrated with the mathematical definitions in Equations 3-4.

### Missing Related Work on Concept-Based Interpretability

- **Insufficient engagement with concept-based representation literature**: The paper lacks adequate discussion of related work on concept-based interpretability for images, which is a significant oversight. The idea of decomposing image representations into interpretable components has been extensively explored in prior work. For example, "Concept Whitening for Interpretable Image Recognition" (Chen et al., 2020) proposes learning concept-aligned subspaces in neural networks, and "Interpretable Basis Decomposition for Visual Explanation" (Zhou et al., 2018) explores how to identify and visualize concept-specific filters in CNNs. More recently, "A Framework to Learn with Interpretation" (Parekh et al., 2021) proposes methods for learning interpretable representations directly. While the present paper's application to generative model evaluation is novel, the underlying technical approach of mapping image representations to semantic concepts has substantial precedent that should be acknowledged and discussed.

- **Limited discussion of attribute selection as a research problem**: The paper treats attribute selection somewhat superficially, dedicating only Section 3.3 to this critical component. However, attribute selection is arguably the most important design choice for the entire framework, as the metrics' interpretability and validity fundamentally depend on having appropriate attributes. The literature on concept-based interpretability has explored this problem extensively, with different approaches to selecting, discovering, or learning attributes. The present paper presents three relatively straightforward approaches (BLIP extraction, user annotation, and GPT-3 generation) but does not deeply investigate their relative merits or limitations. The brief ablation in Figure 5(b) showing that model rankings remain mostly consistent across different numbers of attributes is insufficient; a more thorough investigation of how attribute selection affects metric behavior would be valuable.

### Experimental Limitations

- **Limited scope of model comparisons**: While Section 5.3 compares several generative models, the evaluation is restricted to a handful of models (StyleGAN1, StyleGAN2, StyleGAN2-ADA, StyleGAN3, and iDDPM variants) and only three datasets (FFHQ, LSUN Cat, and MetFaces). To build confidence in the metrics' utility, the paper should include comparisons with a broader range of recent generative models, including more recent diffusion model variants (e.g., Stable Diffusion, DALL-E variants) and other GAN architectures. The limited experimental scope makes it difficult to assess whether the proposed metrics' findings generalize or whether they are specific to particular model families.

- **Insufficient analysis of failure cases**: Section 6 briefly mentions that "unreliable results may arise when the attributes present in the images are ambiguous" and that complex modern artworks present challenges. However, this discussion is too cursory. The paper would benefit from a systematic investigation of when and why the proposed metrics fail. What types of datasets or attributes lead to unreliable DCS scores? How can practitioners identify when the metrics are unreliable? Providing concrete examples and diagnostic procedures would make the metrics more practically useful.

- **Missing ablation studies on key design choices**: The paper does not provide thorough ablation studies on several important design decisions. For instance, why use Gaussian kernel density estimation specifically (mentioned in Section 5)? How sensitive are the results to the choice of kernel? How is the bandwidth parameter selected? Similarly, the choice to use N=20 attributes is not well justified; while Figure 5(b) shows that model rankings are mostly consistent, this does not address whether 20 is optimal or whether the absolute metric values are reliable with this choice.

### Technical and Methodological Concerns

- **Dependency on external models undermines

reviews.summary: The embedding space used for calculating FID is computed with Inception V3 which is trained for image classification as the target task. This means it is more likely to capture discriminative features, raising doubts about its ability to effectively evaluate generative models. There is also a need to devise a new evaluation metric that can interpret underlying factors. This paper introduces a method called Directional CLIP Score (DCS) to properly evaluate this. The pre-trained CLIP is used as the embedding space. In particular, the paper proposes "Single attribute KL div (SaKLD)" to measure single attribute alignment and "Paired attribute KL div (PaKLD)" to measure multiple attribute alignment as new metrics. This paper provides some insightful measurement result using the proposed metrics.
reviews.strengths: The effort to evaluate generative models from various perspectives through the introduction of such metrics can be considered novel and a contribution. Especially considering the current issues such as the bias in stable diffusion [1], the proposal of such metrics can bring benefits to the field from the perspective of trustworthy AI.
reviews.weaknesses:
It appears to be an intrinsic limitation that a significant number of samples (50k) are still required to obtain stable results. Nonetheless, thanks to the reported findings, we can gain more insights, and I appreciate that. 
Remaining concerns are written in [Questions] section.
reviews.questions:
1) In lines 96-97, it seems that the authors of the referenced paper already propose using the CLIP embedding space for evaluating generative model? If not, it would be helpful to clarify the differences compared to previously proposed methods. Regarding the for explainable evaluation in the related work section, it is necessary to determine whether this paper is the first to come up with it. If there are any additional previous works, it would be helpful to share the, 

2) The major concern raised is the reliance on the embedding space. What if there are biases in the CLIP space itself? For example, if being female and wearing makeup are highly correlated, even a woman who does not actually wear makeup may show high similarity to makeup attribute. In such cases, it becomes difficult to consider CLIP score or CLIP direction score as accurate measures of similarity. While a more disentangled multimodal latent space may help alleviate this problem, I'm curious about the authors' perspective on this issue.

3) Directional CLIP Score adopts a method of computing the center of training images and normalizing them. Does this mean that anyone who wants to evaluate a generative model needs to share all these training images? Also, considering the introduction of the auxiliary image captioning model called BLIP, the improvement in performance seems minimal. Do the authors believe it is worth the trade-off?

4) Question about Table 5: cI may have missed it, but was there an explanation for the last column of FID_clip in Table 5? What does it represent? Also, in Table 5, it seems that SaKLD is calculated for a single attribute. How is it computed?

5) Can PaKLD be proposed for more than two attributes? It would be great if judgments could be made for a wide range of attributes as shown in Figure 1 (b).

6) How should we interpret numerical values itself of the scores?  Although it may not be as interpretable as accuracy I'm still curious about the authors' insights based on their experience.
reviews.limitations: It seems that there are no particular specific limitations.

reviews.text:
# Expanded Review: Attribute Based Interpretable Evaluation Metrics for Generative Models

## Summary

The embedding space used for calculating FID is computed with Inception V3, which is trained for image classification as the target task. This architectural choice means it is more likely to capture discriminative features optimized for classification boundaries rather than generative quality, raising fundamental doubts about its ability to effectively evaluate generative models across diverse domains and tasks. The authors correctly identify that there is a critical need to devise new evaluation metrics that can interpret the underlying factors behind judgments, moving beyond opaque numerical scores that provide no insight into model behavior. 

This paper introduces a method called Directional CLIP Score (DCS) to address these concerns by properly evaluating generative models through an interpretable embedding space. Rather than relying on classification-oriented embeddings, the paper leverages pre-trained CLIP, a vision-language model trained on diverse web data, as the foundation for the embedding space. Building on this foundation, the paper proposes two novel metrics: "Single attribute KL divergence (SaKLD)" to measure alignment of individual attribute distributions between training and generated data, and "Paired attribute KL divergence (PaKLD)" to measure whether the model preserves relationships between multiple attributes. The paper provides insightful measurement results using the proposed metrics, comparing prominent generative models including StyleGAN variants and diffusion-based approaches.

## Strengths

The effort to evaluate generative models from multiple interpretable perspectives through the introduction of such metrics represents a novel and valuable contribution to the field. The motivation is well-articulated through concrete examples (Figure 1) that clearly demonstrate how existing metrics fail to provide explanations for their judgments. This interpretability-focused approach is particularly timely and important, especially considering documented issues such as the gender and racial biases in stable diffusion and other generative models. The proposal of interpretable evaluation metrics can bring significant benefits to the field from the perspective of trustworthy AI and model transparency.

The technical approach is sound and well-motivated. The transition from standard CLIPScore to Directional CLIPScore is well-justified through both intuitive geometric reasoning (Figure 3) and empirical validation (Table 1), demonstrating that DCS achieves superior accuracy (0.530 vs. 0.501 mean accuracy on refined CelebA attributes) compared to raw CLIPScore. The paper's design of three different attribute selection methodologies (BLIP-extracted, user annotation, and GPT-based) shows thoughtful consideration of practical deployment scenarios where different users may have different attribute priorities.

The experimental validation is comprehensive and well-designed. The correlated image injection experiment (Section 5.1) provides a clever validation approach that directly tests whether the metrics can detect attribute distribution shifts. The toy experiment in Section 5.2 effectively demonstrates a concrete scenario where SaKLD fails but PaKLD succeeds, providing clear evidence for the necessity of considering paired attributes. The comparative analysis of GANs versus diffusion models (Tables 3-4) yields interesting insights, such as the observation that diffusion models struggle particularly with color attributes, which the authors connect to optimal transport theory. The ablation studies examining the effects of sample size and attribute count (Figure 5) provide useful practical guidance for practitioners using these metrics.

## Weaknesses and Concerns

### Fundamental Sample Size Requirements

A significant practical limitation is that stable results require approximately 50,000 samples, as demonstrated in Figure 5(a). This is a substantial requirement that may limit applicability in scenarios with smaller datasets or computational constraints. While the authors acknowledge this finding and use 50k samples in their main experiments, the implications deserve more discussion. This requirement is actually larger than many existing datasets used in practice, and it raises questions about whether practitioners with more limited resources can reliably apply these metrics. The convergence behavior shown in Figure 5(a) suggests that the kernel density estimation approach may be sensitive to sample size, though the authors do not explore whether alternative density estimation methods might improve convergence properties.

### Critical Concerns Regarding CLIP Embedding Space Biases

The major concern raised is the fundamental reliance on the CLIP embedding space itself, which may contain its own biases and correlations that are not necessarily desirable for evaluation purposes. The authors do not adequately address what happens if there are inherent biases in CLIP's representation space. For example, if CLIP's training data contains strong correlations between being female and wearing makeup (as is common in web-scraped datasets), then even a woman who does not actually wear makeup may show high similarity to the makeup attribute in CLIP's embedding space. This would cause the DCS metric to incorrectly attribute makeup to images where it is not present, leading to systematic evaluation errors.

This concern is particularly important because it calls into question whether the proposed metrics truly measure what they claim to measure, or whether they simply transfer biases from CLIP to the evaluation process. The paper would benefit from empirical analysis examining potential correlations in CLIP's embedding space and how these might affect attribute measurements. Additionally, the authors should discuss whether debiasing techniques from the literature on fair machine learning could be applied to improve the CLIP embedding space before using it for evaluation. Without addressing these concerns, the claimed interpretability advantage over FID is somewhat undermined—the metrics are more interpretable in the sense that they explicitly reference attributes, but the accuracy of those measurements remains questionable.

### Practical Concerns About Attribute Selection and BLIP Integration

The three attribute selection methodologies introduce different practical challenges that are not fully explored. The BLIP-extracted attributes approach requires automatic caption generation, but Figure 2 and the discussion suggest that BLIP may introduce its own biases in what attributes it detects. The improvement from CLIP Score to Directional CLIP Score is modest (from 0.395 to 0.409 without BLIP refinement, and from 0.501 to 0.530 with refined attributes), raising questions about whether the added complexity of BLIP integration is justified by the performance gains.

More fundamentally, the Directional CLIP Score computation requires access to the center of training images (CX) and the center of attribute text embeddings (CT), as shown in Equation 2. This raises a practical concern: does this mean that anyone who wants to evaluate a generative model using DCS needs access to all the training images used to compute these centers? This could be problematic for proprietary datasets or in scenarios where training data cannot be shared. The paper should clarify whether these centers must be recomputed for each new evaluation scenario, or whether they can be cached and reused. If they must be recomputed, this significantly limits the metric's practical applicability compared to FID, which only requires the pre-trained Inception network.

### Questions About Metric Interpretation and Scope

**Question 1: Novelty and Positioning Relative to Prior Work**
In lines 96-97, it appears that the referenced work (Kynkäänniemi et al.) already proposes using CLIP embedding space for evaluating generative models. It would be helpful to clarify the specific differences between this paper's approach and that prior work. Additionally, regarding explainable evaluation metrics in the related work section, it is necessary to determine whether this paper is the first to propose attribute-based interpretable evaluation. If there are additional previous works addressing explainability in generative model evaluation, it would strengthen the paper to discuss them and clarify the novel contributions.

**Question 2: Limitations of the Paired Attribute Approach**
While PaKLD is proposed for pairs of attributes, the paper does not address whether this can be extended to larger attribute combinations. Can PaKLD be computed for triplets of attributes or larger combinations? The current limitation to pairs may miss important higher-order interactions between attributes. The paper would be strengthened by discussing whether and how the approach could scale to capture richer attribute relationships, as suggested by the multi-attribute scenario in Figure 1(b).

**Question 3: Numerical Score Interpretation**
The paper does not provide clear guidance on how to interpret the numerical values of SaKLD and PaKLD scores. Although these metrics are more interpretable than FID in the sense that they explicitly reference attributes, the absolute values themselves remain difficult to interpret. What does a SaKLD value of 10.0 versus 15.0 mean in practical terms? How should practitioners decide whether a difference in scores is meaningful? The paper would benefit from providing interpretation guidelines, perhaps by establishing score ranges that correspond to different levels of attribute distribution mismatch. Without this guidance, practitioners may struggle to use these metrics effectively in practice.

**Question 4: Table 2 and Metric Calculation Details**
In Table 2, the column labeled "FID_CLIP" appears without clear explanation of what it represents. Additionally, it would be helpful to have more detailed explanation of how SaKLD is computed when considering multiple attributes—the paper states it averages KL divergences across attributes (Equation 5), but more intuition about whether this simple averaging is appropriate would strengthen the presentation. Are all attributes equally important, or should some attributes be weighted more heavily than others?

### Limited Discussion of Failure Cases

The authors briefly mention in Section 6 that "unreliable results may arise when the attributes present in the images are ambiguous" and provide the example of complex modern

This paper provides some insightful measurement result using the proposed metrics.

Strengths:
The effort to evaluate generative models from various perspectives through the introduction of such metrics can be considered novel and a contribution. Especially considering the current issues such as the bias in stable diffusion [1], the proposal of such metrics can bring benefits to the field from the perspective of trustworthy AI.

Weaknesses:
It appears to be an intrinsic limitation that a significant number of samples (50k) are still required to obtain stable results. Nonetheless, thanks to the reported findings, we can gain more insights, and I appreciate that. 
Remaining concerns are written in [Questions] section.

Questions:
1) In lines 96-97, it seems that the authors of the referenced paper already propose using the CLIP embedding space for evaluating generative model? If not, it would be helpful to clarify the differences compared to previously proposed methods. Regarding the for explainable evaluation in the related work section, it is necessary to determine whether this paper is the first to come up with it. If there are any additional previous works, it would be helpful to share the, 

2) The major concern raised is the reliance on the embedding space. What if there are biases in the CLIP space itself? For example, if being female and wearing makeup are highly correlated, even a woman who does not actually wear makeup may show high similarity to makeup attribute. In such cases, it becomes difficult to consider CLIP score or CLIP direction score as accurate measures of similarity. While a more disentangled multimodal latent space may help alleviate this problem, I'm curious about the authors' perspective on this issue.

3) Directional CLIP Score adopts a method of computing the center of training images and normalizing them. Does this mean that anyone who wants to evaluate a generative model needs to share all these training images? Also, considering the introduction of the auxiliary image captioning model called BLIP, the improvement in performance seems minimal. Do the authors believe it is worth the trade-off?

4) Question about Table 5: cI may have missed it, but was there an explanation for the last column of FID_clip in Table 5? What does it represent? Also, in Table 5, it seems that SaKLD is calculated for a single attribute. How is it computed?

5) Can PaKLD be proposed for more than two attributes? It would be great if judgments could be made for a wide range of attributes as shown in Figure 1 (b).

6) How should we interpret numerical values itself of the scores?  Although it may not be as interpretable as accuracy I'm still curious about the authors' insights based on their experience.

Limitations:
It seems that there are no particular specific limitations.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.original_rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This is a very interesting research work. The main contribution of this paper is to consider the attribute information in the original training data when evaluating the quality of images generated by the model. There are two benefits to this approach: 1) determining whether the model can correctly imitate the distribution of the training data; 2) explaining which attributes the model does not perform well on. In implementing this idea, the authors found that directly calculating the CLIPScore between the image representation and the text representation of the attribute does not yield distinctive results. Therefore, they proposed Directional CLIPScore (DSC). The main idea of this approach is to move the reference point for calculating vector similarity to a more reasonable point. At the same time, they proposed two methods to apply DSC, one is Single attribute KL Divergence, and the other is Paired attribute KL Divergence (PaKLD) considering the combination of attributes.
reviews.strengths:
1. I think this research question is very interesting. It is very meaningful to use the attribute information of the dataset to help evaluate the quality of the model's generation.
2. The Directional CLIPScore (DSC) proposed by the authors is very concise and appears to be quite effective in the case study.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. In Section 3.3, I agree with the extraction of attributes using the BLIP and manual annotation methods. However, one method of extracting attributes is to generate an attribute list with GPT and then filter it with training data. I think the attribute list generated by GPT in advance may bring biases. The core of this paper is mainly to study the correlation between the model and the training data. However, if the list generated by GPT is not the most representative attribute, the results may be biased.
2. The experimental part in Sections 5.1-5.3 does not seem very convincing. The authors mainly verify that the proposed method can indeed be consistent with some expected experimental designs, but there is a lack of more convincing quantitative indicators to show that their proposed evaluation metrics are better than those proposed by others previous research works. I would prefer to see the authors analyze the correlation coefficient between their proposed evaluation metrics and human evaluation, as well as whether their evaluation metrics have improved in terms of correlation coefficient compared to previous evaluation indicators. This is my biggest concern for this work.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This is a very interesting research work. The main contribution of this paper is to consider the attribute information in the original training data when evaluating the quality of images generated by the model. There are two benefits to this approach: 1) determining whether the model can correctly imitate the distribution of the training data; 2) explaining which attributes the model does not perform well on. In implementing this idea, the authors found that directly calculating the CLIPScore between the image representation and the text representation of the attribute does not yield distinctive results. Therefore, they proposed Directional CLIPScore (DSC). The main idea of this approach is to move the reference point for calculating vector similarity to a more reasonable point. At the same time, they proposed two methods to apply DSC, one is Single attribute KL Divergence, and the other is Paired attribute KL Divergence (PaKLD) considering the combination of attributes.

Strengths:
1. I think this research question is very interesting. It is very meaningful to use the attribute information of the dataset to help evaluate the quality of the model's generation.
2. The Directional CLIPScore (DSC) proposed by the authors is very concise and appears to be quite effective in the case study.

Weaknesses:
1. In Section 3.3, I agree with the extraction of attributes using the BLIP and manual annotation methods. However, one method of extracting attributes is to generate an attribute list with GPT and then filter it with training data. I think the attribute list generated by GPT in advance may bring biases. The core of this paper is mainly to study the correlation between the model and the training data. However, if the list generated by GPT is not the most representative attribute, the results may be biased.
2. The experimental part in Sections 5.1-5.3 does not seem very convincing. The authors mainly verify that the proposed method can indeed be consistent with some expected experimental designs, but there is a lack of more convincing quantitative indicators to show that their proposed evaluation metrics are better than those proposed by others previous research works. I would prefer to see the authors analyze the correlation coefficient between their proposed evaluation metrics and human evaluation, as well as whether their evaluation metrics have improved in terms of correlation coefficient compared to previous evaluation indicators. This is my biggest concern for this work.

Questions:
1. In Section 3.1, can the problem exhibited by CLIPScore be solved by directly calculating the Euclidean distance between E(x) and E(a) without relying on Directional CLIPScore?
2. Notation overload. The meanings of N used in Eq. (5) and Eq. (2) are different.

Limitations:
see weakness

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary

This is a very interesting and timely research work that addresses an important gap in the field of generative model evaluation. The main contribution of this paper is to incorporate attribute information from the original training data as a central component of the evaluation framework for assessing the quality of generated images. This approach offers two significant benefits: 1) determining whether the model can correctly imitate the distribution of the training data across multiple semantic dimensions; 2) providing interpretable explanations of which specific attributes the model does not perform well on, moving beyond opaque numerical scores. 

In implementing this idea, the authors encountered a practical challenge: directly calculating the CLIPScore between image representations and text representations of attributes does not yield sufficiently distinctive results to discriminate between different levels of attribute presence. To address this limitation, they proposed Directional CLIPScore (DCS), an elegant refinement of the original CLIPScore metric. The key insight of this approach is to shift the reference point for calculating vector similarity from the origin to a more contextually appropriate point—specifically, the center of the training data distribution. This modification is conceptually simple yet appears to be quite effective in practice, as demonstrated in Table 1 where DCS achieves mean accuracy of 0.530 compared to CLIPScore's 0.409 on refined CelebA attributes. Building on this foundation, the authors proposed two complementary metrics: Single attribute KL Divergence (SaKLD) for assessing individual attribute distributions, and Paired attribute KL Divergence (PaKLD) for capturing the preservation of relationships between attributes, recognizing that attributes in real data are often correlated and these correlations carry important semantic information.

## Strengths

1. **Novel and well-motivated research question**: The paper addresses a genuine and important limitation in current evaluation practices for generative models. The motivation is clearly articulated through the compelling example in Figure 1, which effectively demonstrates that while existing metrics like FID can identify poor performance, they provide no interpretable explanation for their judgments. The observation that humans readily identify biases (e.g., "Model 2 generates only women") but current metrics fail to explain such biases is particularly compelling and motivates the need for attribute-based evaluation. This gap between metric scores and human interpretability is a real problem that practitioners face when analyzing generative model outputs.

2. **Elegant and well-justified technical approach**: The Directional CLIPScore (DCS) is a concise yet effective refinement of existing methods. The conceptual motivation—that attributes in the CLIP embedding space cluster densely together, resulting in similar cosine similarity values—is clearly explained with supporting visualization in Figure 3. The proposed solution of using centered vectors (Equations 3-4) is intuitive and grounded in geometric reasoning. The empirical validation in Table 1 demonstrates concrete improvements, with DCS achieving higher accuracy (0.530 vs 0.409 for refined attributes) than standard CLIPScore. The authors also provide thoughtful discussion of why this approach works better, relating it to the properties of the CLIP embedding space and the need for a meaningful reference point.

3. **Comprehensive exploration of attribute selection strategies**: The paper thoughtfully considers multiple approaches to attribute selection (BLIP-extracted, user annotation, and GPT-based), recognizing that different use cases may benefit from different strategies. This flexibility is valuable for practitioners who may have different constraints and requirements. The inclusion of user-annotated attributes (leveraging CelebA's 40 binary attributes) provides a well-established baseline, while BLIP-extracted attributes offer an automated, dataset-specific alternative.

4. **Well-designed validation experiments**: The correlated image injection experiment (Section 5.1) and the toy experiment demonstrating the necessity of PaKLD (Section 5.2) are thoughtfully constructed. The injection experiment is particularly clever—by systematically adding edited images with specific attribute combinations (e.g., "man" with "makeup") to one subset, the authors create a controlled scenario where the expected behavior of the metrics is clear and verifiable. The results in Table 2 showing that both SaKLD and PaKLD increase monotonically with the injection of correlated images provide concrete evidence that the metrics behave as intended. The toy experiment in Section 5.2 is equally well-designed, clearly illustrating a scenario where SaKLD fails but PaKLD succeeds, thereby demonstrating the complementary nature of the two proposed metrics.

5. **Interesting comparative analysis of GANs vs. diffusion models**: The observation that StyleGAN2-ADA performs worse than expected despite FID's superiority, and that diffusion models underperform GANs on attribute-based metrics, provides fresh insights into model behavior. The follow-up analysis in Table 4 attributing diffusion models' weakness to color attributes is particularly insightful, connecting the empirical observations to theoretical understanding via the optimal transport interpretation (Khrulkov et al.). This kind of deeper analysis demonstrates the diagnostic potential of attribute-based metrics beyond simple ranking.

## Weaknesses

1. **Potential bias in GPT-based attribute selection**: In Section 3.3, while the extraction of attributes using BLIP and manual annotation methods is well-justified, the GPT-based attribute extraction approach raises concerns about introduced biases. The authors generate an initial attribute list through GPT by asking questions like "Give me 50 words of useful visual attributes for distinguishing faces in a photo," and then filter this list using training data. However, the initial GPT-generated list may carry inherent biases reflecting the training data and design choices of GPT-3, which could systematically favor certain types of attributes over others. Since the core premise of this paper is to study the alignment between the model's output distribution and the training data distribution, using a pre-filtered attribute list that may not be the most representative of the training data's semantic dimensions could introduce systematic biases into the evaluation results.

   Furthermore, the paper does not provide detailed analysis of how the three attribute selection methods compare in terms of the attributes they identify or how sensitive the final metric rankings are to the choice of attribute selection method. While Figure 5(b) shows that model rankings "mostly remained consistent" across different numbers of attributes, this does not directly address whether the choice between BLIP, user annotation, and GPT methods produces substantially different results. A more thorough comparison of these three methods would strengthen the paper's contribution and help practitioners understand when to use which approach.

2. **Limited validation against human judgment**: The experimental section (Sections 5.1-5.3) primarily validates that the proposed methods behave consistently with expected experimental designs, but there is a significant lack of quantitative comparison against human evaluation. The authors verify that their metrics respond appropriately to injected correlated images and that PaKLD captures attribute relationships that SaKLD misses, but these are largely internal consistency checks. What is missing is a more convincing demonstration that the proposed evaluation metrics better align with human judgment compared to existing metrics like FID, Precision/Recall, and other prior work.

   This is a critical concern because the paper's primary motivation—that existing metrics fail to provide interpretable explanations—implicitly assumes that humans would prefer attribute-based explanations. A rigorous validation would involve collecting human evaluations of generated image sets and computing correlation coefficients between human judgments and both the proposed metrics and existing metrics. This would provide quantitative evidence that the proposed approach actually improves upon existing methods in terms of capturing what matters to human evaluators. Without such validation, it remains unclear whether the interpretability gained through attribute-based metrics translates to metrics that are actually more predictive of perceptual quality or diversity as judged by humans.

   The paper would be substantially strengthened by including a human evaluation study, even if limited in scope, where human raters evaluate generated image sets and their judgments are correlated with both the new metrics and existing baselines. Such a study could focus on a subset of the datasets and models tested, but would provide crucial evidence for the practical utility of the proposed approach.

3. **Incomplete analysis of metric sensitivity and robustness**: While Section 5.4 provides ablation studies on the number of samples and attributes, the paper lacks deeper investigation into the robustness and sensitivity of the proposed metrics. For instance, how sensitive are SaKLD and PaKLD to the choice of kernel density estimation bandwidth? How do the metrics perform when the training data has imbalanced attribute distributions? The paper mentions that "unreliable results may arise when the attributes present in the images are ambiguous" (Section 6), but this limitation is not thoroughly explored or quantified.

   Additionally, the choice of Gaussian kernel density estimation for estimating PDFs is not justified. Why not use other density estimation methods? How sensitive are the results to this choice? The paper samples 10,000 points from each PDF to obtain discretized distributions, but the impact of this discretization on the final metric values is not analyzed. These technical details, while seemingly minor, can significantly affect the reliability and reproducibility of the proposed metrics.

4. **Limited scope of experimental validation**: The experiments focus primarily on face generation datasets (FFHQ, CelebA-HQ, MetFaces) and one category dataset (LSUN Cat). While faces are an important domain for generative modeling, the generalizability of the proposed approach to
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This is a very interesting research work. The main contribution of this paper is to consider the attribute information in the original training data when evaluating the quality of images generated by the model. There are two benefits to this approach: 1) determining whether the model can correctly imitate the distribution of the training data; 2) explaining which attributes the model does not perform well on. In implementing this idea, the authors found that directly calculating the CLIPScore between the image representation and the text representation of the attribute does not yield distinctive results. Therefore, they proposed Directional CLIPScore (DSC). The main idea of this approach is to move the reference point for calculating vector similarity to a more reasonable point. At the same time, they proposed two methods to apply DSC, one is Single attribute KL Divergence, and the other is Paired attribute KL Divergence (PaKLD) considering the combination of attributes.

Strengths:
1. I think this research question is very interesting. It is very meaningful to use the attribute information of the dataset to help evaluate the quality of the model's generation.
2. The Directional CLIPScore (DSC) proposed by the authors is very concise and appears to be quite effective in the case study.

Weaknesses:
1. In Section 3.3, I agree with the extraction of attributes using the BLIP and manual annotation methods. However, one method of extracting attributes is to generate an attribute list with GPT and then filter it with training data. I think the attribute list generated by GPT in advance may bring biases. The core of this paper is mainly to study the correlation between the model and the training data. However, if the list generated by GPT is not the most representative attribute, the results may be biased.
2. The experimental part in Sections 5.1-5.3 does not seem very convincing. The authors mainly verify that the proposed method can indeed be consistent with some expected experimental designs, but there is a lack of more convincing quantitative indicators to show that their proposed evaluation metrics are better than those proposed by others previous research works. I would prefer to see the authors analyze the correlation coefficient between their proposed evaluation metrics and human evaluation, as well as whether their evaluation metrics have improved in terms of correlation coefficient compared to previous evaluation indicators. This is my biggest concern for this work.

Questions:
1. In Section 3.1, can the problem exhibited by CLIPScore be solved by directly calculating the Euclidean distance between E(x) and E(a) without relying on Directional CLIPScore?
2. Notation overload. The meanings of N used in Eq. (5) and Eq. (2) are different.

Limitations:
see weakness

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

reviews.summary:
This paper proposes a new metric to evaluate the quality and diversity of generated images based on
interpretable embeddings. To obtain the interpretable embeddings for selected attributes, the cluster
centers of the encodings of two separate encoders, one for images and the other for the text attributes
are calculated and the interpretable embeddings are the difference between the encodings of each
image and attribute and their respective cluster centers. The interpretable embeddings represent the
direction in which a particular embedding lies with respect to it’s cluster center. The CLIPScore
between the interpretable embeddings of an image and an attribute is calculated and is named as
’Directional CLIPScore’, since the interpretable embeddings represent the ’direction’.
The authors have proposed two metrics : 1. SaKLD - to quatify how closely the attribute distri-
bution in generated images matches with that of training images. 2. PaKLD - to quantify correlations
between different attributes. The KL divergence between the probability density functions of the
Directional CLIPScores for all the images in the train data and generated data.
Using SaKLD, the KL divergence between the attribute distribution in training and generated
images is calculated. PaKLD calculates the KL divergence similar to SaKLD, except that the presence
of a pair of attributes is required in the training and generated images.
reviews.strengths: The motivation for the idea is good and has a huge potential impact for improving evaluation of generative models.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Although, the motivation for developing this metric is valid, the overall methodology and the
experimental results are not convincing for the use of this metric. Also, the experiments performed
are inadequate and do not sufficiently justify how well the metric performs compared to the previously
proposed metrics. Additionally, previous metrics can directly evaluate quality of generation based
on the generated images alone, but this metric heavily relies on the attributes in the form of text
descriptions. Thus, it limits the applicability and generalizability of this metric.
- The proposed metrics use CLIPScore (which already exists) for interpretable embeddings and then
applies KL Divergence for the PDFs of the ClipScores of images and attributes, thus, showing limited
novelty.
- Most of the paper is easy to follow but some important parts like, how is the center of text attributes
calculated, results from table 1 (what does accuracy stand for) etc. are a bit ambiguous. There is a
lot of scope to improve the technical soundness of the paper. Although some of the popular metrics
1are mentioned, there has been a lot of work in the generative modelling domain which the literature
survey must cover. The proposed methodology is not very sound and is not well supported with the
experimental setup. The results are also not sufficiently explained. Diversity is the main motivation
for the paper as it is mentioned in the abstract but any theoretical or empirical work to support it is
completely missing.
- Motivation and methodology including the steps to evaluate generated samples is understandable but
some parts are ambiguous (please refer above comments).
- Based on the current state of the experiments, the contributions don’t seem to be significant as there
is not enough validation to support the claims.

 typos:
- Line 43 : Instead of ”Figure 1 (b)”, it should be Figure 1 (a)
- Line 168 : Section 3.3 First letters of all the words in the heading must be capital
Other remarks :
- Line 56 : Instead of ”If the model lacks essential attributes” the following sounds technically
correct ”if model lacks ”representation” of essential attributes”.
- Line 56-58 : This claim does not seem to be correct.
- Line 147 : Link to the specified figure is missing
- Figure 2 is never refered to in the text, is it an unnecessary figure?
- Line 253 : Generated set has non-smiling men and non-smiling women. But Figure 4 caption
says otherwise
reviews.questions:
1. If I understood correctly, the text and the image encoders are different? If yes, then
the embedding space of both models is going to be very different and will be influenced by the
downstream tasks that they are trained on. Would this metric always work for embeddings from
different models? Does it make sense to calculate similarity between embeddings from different
models?
2. Table 1 represents accuracy results for CS vs DCS. What is meant by evaluating ”positive
samples” in line 164 and 165? And what is meant by ”how well they are aligned”? Also, what
exactly is the accuracy calculated for?
3. In Section 3.1, there is a mention of ”each channel of the embedding being utilized”, how exactly
does this happen?
4. Line 196-197 claims that the generated distribution is desirable only if it has exactly same number
of samples as in the training set that has a particular attribute. Why should it be? Afaik, the
underlying distribution of latents represent semantic features of the data and the generative
model is responsible to generate novel samples that could be realistic but not necessarily show
exactly same features same number of times in the training set. So, is the assumption in the
mentioned lines appropriate? Can you prove this or maybe refer to some papers that provide
such guarantees?
reviews.limitations: limitations have been addressed
reviews.rating: 4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.

reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review: Attribute Based Interpretable Evaluation Metrics for Generative Models

## Summary

This paper proposes a new metric to evaluate the quality and diversity of generated images based on interpretable embeddings derived from CLIP encoders. The core methodological contribution involves calculating cluster centers of the encodings from separate image and text encoders, with interpretable embeddings defined as the difference between individual encodings and their respective cluster centers. This directional representation is termed 'Directional CLIPScore' (DCS) since the interpretable embeddings represent the direction in which a particular embedding lies with respect to its cluster center.

The authors propose two primary metrics: (1) Single attribute KL Divergence (SaKLD), which quantifies how closely the attribute distribution in generated images matches that of training images by computing KL divergence between probability density functions of DCS values; and (2) Paired attribute KL Divergence (PaKLD), which quantifies correlations between different attributes by calculating KL divergence for joint distributions of attribute pairs. The paper demonstrates these metrics on several state-of-the-art generative models including StyleGAN variants and diffusion models across multiple datasets.

## Strengths

The motivation for developing interpretable evaluation metrics is compelling and addresses a genuine gap in the current generative modeling evaluation landscape. The paper correctly identifies that existing metrics like FID, Precision/Recall, and Density/Coverage provide limited insight into *why* a model performs better or worse, offering only numerical scores without actionable explanations. This observation is well-articulated in Figure 1, which effectively illustrates how existing metrics fail to explain that Model 2's inferiority stems from bias toward specific attributes like "long hair" and "makeup."

The fundamental insight that evaluation metrics should provide interpretable explanations aligned with human judgment is sound and has significant potential impact for improving the field's understanding of generative model behavior. The paper's focus on attribute-level analysis resonates with practical concerns in generative modeling, where practitioners often care about whether models generate diverse representations across demographic groups, object types, and visual characteristics.

The experimental design in Section 5.1 (correlated image injection) is thoughtfully constructed, providing a controlled validation scenario where the proposed metrics should clearly capture introduced distribution shifts. Similarly, the toy experiment in Section 5.2 effectively demonstrates a concrete failure case of SaKLD while showing PaKLD's superiority, which helps justify the need for both metrics.

## Major Weaknesses

### Limited Novelty and Methodological Concerns

The proposed metrics demonstrate limited technical novelty, as they primarily combine existing components: CLIPScore (which already exists, as acknowledged via reference [8]) and standard KL Divergence calculations. The core contribution—computing DCS by subtracting cluster centers before calculating similarity—is a relatively straightforward modification to CLIPScore. While this modification shows empirical improvements in Table 1 (DCS mean accuracy of 0.530 vs CLIPScore's 0.501 on refined attributes), the improvement is modest and the theoretical justification for why this specific transformation should work better remains underdeveloped.

The paper does not provide sufficient theoretical analysis of why directional similarity relative to cluster centers should be superior for attribute detection beyond the intuitive explanation that "cosine similarity values tend to be similar" (line 148). A deeper investigation into the geometric properties of the CLIP embedding space and formal analysis of why centering improves discriminability would strengthen the contribution. Additionally, the relationship between DCS and existing normalization techniques in machine learning is not discussed.

### Fundamental Assumption About Attribute Distribution

A critical and largely unexamined assumption underlies both proposed metrics: that a well-trained generative model should produce exactly the same distribution of attributes as the training data. The paper states this explicitly at lines 196-197: "For example, if 50,000 training data contains 3,000 images with eyeglasses, the model should generate exactly 3,000 images with eyeglasses. Any deviation from this ideal distribution is considered undesirable."

This assumption is problematic for several reasons that the paper does not adequately address:

1. **Theoretical Justification Missing**: Generative models are trained to learn the underlying data distribution, not to memorize exact attribute frequencies. The latent space of generative models is designed to capture semantic features and variations, not to enforce exact attribute matching. The paper provides no theoretical foundation or empirical evidence supporting why this assumption is appropriate. Can the authors cite literature establishing that attribute distribution matching is a necessary condition for good generative models?

2. **Contradiction with Generative Modeling Theory**: Modern generative models (GANs, diffusion models) optimize for distributional similarity in learned feature spaces, not for exact preservation of all attributes. A model that generates novel, realistic samples with slightly different attribute distributions than the training set might still be considered superior by practitioners, yet this metric would penalize such a model.

3. **Practical Implications**: The assumption implies that any deviation from training attribute frequencies is undesirable. However, practitioners often deliberately seek models that can generate diverse samples beyond the training distribution or that correct for imbalances in the training data. The metric provides no flexibility for such use cases.

The paper would benefit from either providing rigorous justification for this assumption or reformulating the metrics to be more flexible about attribute distribution matching.

### Inadequate Experimental Validation

While the paper includes several experiments, the experimental validation is insufficient to support the central claims:

1. **Limited Comparison with Existing Metrics**: The paper primarily compares against FID and briefly mentions FID_CLIP, but does not comprehensively compare against other interpretability-focused or attribute-based evaluation methods. The related work section (Section 2) acknowledges existing work on precision/recall and density/coverage, yet these metrics are not experimentally compared against the proposed approach. A fair evaluation would include quantitative comparisons showing when and why the proposed metrics outperform existing alternatives.

2. **Lack of Human Evaluation**: Given that the paper's central motivation is alignment with human judgment (line 65: "humans readily discern certain factors for judgment"), the complete absence of human evaluation studies is a significant gap. The paper would be substantially strengthened by conducting human studies where evaluators rank generated image sets, and then correlating these human rankings with both existing metrics and the proposed metrics. This would provide empirical validation of the claim that these metrics better align with human judgment.

3. **Inconsistent and Unexplained Results**: Table 3 presents concerning findings that are not adequately explained:
   - StyleGAN2-ADA performs worse than StyleGAN2 despite being designed as an improvement, which contradicts the literature. The paper mentions in the appendix that "data augmentation for generative models may ruin attribute distribution," but this claim requires more rigorous investigation and explanation.
   - iDDPM performs significantly worse than GAN-based methods across all datasets, which is surprising given the recent success of diffusion models. While Section 5.3 offers a hypothesis about color attributes and optimal transport maps (referencing Khrulkov et al. [15]), this explanation is speculative and not empirically validated through ablation studies or visualization.
   - The paper does not explain why these results would be considered valid or trustworthy given these inconsistencies.

4. **Insufficient Analysis of Attribute Selection Methods**: The paper proposes three attribute selection strategies (BLIP-extracted, user-annotated, and GPT-based) but does not thoroughly compare their effects. Table 2 shows all three methods together but lacks detailed analysis of when each method is appropriate. The differences between methods are non-trivial (e.g., BLIP USER vs GPT in Table 2 show substantial score variations), yet the paper provides minimal guidance on method selection.

5. **Missing Ablation Studies**: The paper lacks ablations on key design choices. For example: How sensitive are the metrics to the choice of kernel density estimation bandwidth? What happens if different clustering algorithms are used to compute centers? How do results change with different numbers of samples (Figure 5a shows stabilization around 50k, but why is this threshold appropriate)?

### Dependency on Text Attributes Limits Generalizability

A fundamental limitation that significantly reduces the practical applicability of this approach is the heavy reliance on pre-defined text attribute descriptions. This creates several problems:

1. **Domain Specificity**: The metrics are designed and validated primarily on face datasets (FFHQ, CelebA, MetFaces) where clear, well-defined attributes exist. The paper does not demonstrate effectiveness on other domains. For example, how would one define appropriate attributes for abstract art, medical imaging, or complex scene generation? The paper briefly acknowledges this limitation (lines 306-308: "unreliable results may arise when the attributes present in the images are ambiguous"), but this is a severe constraint on generalizability.

2. **Attribute Definition Bias**: The choice of attributes fundamentally shapes the evaluation results. Different attribute sets lead to different rankings of models (as shown in Figure 5b where scores vary with attribute count). This introduces a subjective element that contradicts the paper's goal of objective evaluation. Unlike FID which uses a fixed pre-trained network, the proposed metrics require domain-specific human decisions about which attributes matter.

3. **
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a new metric to evaluate the quality and diversity of generated images based on
interpretable embeddings. To obtain the interpretable embeddings for selected attributes, the cluster
centers of the encodings of two separate encoders, one for images and the other for the text attributes
are calculated and the interpretable embeddings are the difference between the encodings of each
image and attribute and their respective cluster centers. The interpretable embeddings represent the
direction in which a particular embedding lies with respect to it’s cluster center. The CLIPScore
between the interpretable embeddings of an image and an attribute is calculated and is named as
’Directional CLIPScore’, since the interpretable embeddings represent the ’direction’.
The authors have proposed two metrics : 1. SaKLD - to quatify how closely the attribute distri-
bution in generated images matches with that of training images. 2. PaKLD - to quantify correlations
between different attributes. The KL divergence between the probability density functions of the
Directional CLIPScores for all the images in the train data and generated data.
Using SaKLD, the KL divergence between the attribute distribution in training and generated
images is calculated. PaKLD calculates the KL divergence similar to SaKLD, except that the presence
of a pair of attributes is required in the training and generated images.

Strengths:
The motivation for the idea is good and has a huge potential impact for improving evaluation of generative models.

Weaknesses:
- Although, the motivation for developing this metric is valid, the overall methodology and the
experimental results are not convincing for the use of this metric. Also, the experiments performed
are inadequate and do not sufficiently justify how well the metric performs compared to the previously
proposed metrics. Additionally, previous metrics can directly evaluate quality of generation based
on the generated images alone, but this metric heavily relies on the attributes in the form of text
descriptions. Thus, it limits the applicability and generalizability of this metric.
- The proposed metrics use CLIPScore (which already exists) for interpretable embeddings and then
applies KL Divergence for the PDFs of the ClipScores of images and attributes, thus, showing limited
novelty.
- Most of the paper is easy to follow but some important parts like, how is the center of text attributes
calculated, results from table 1 (what does accuracy stand for) etc. are a bit ambiguous. There is a
lot of scope to improve the technical soundness of the paper. Although some of the popular metrics
1are mentioned, there has been a lot of work in the generative modelling domain which the literature
survey must cover. The proposed methodology is not very sound and is not well supported with the
experimental setup. The results are also not sufficiently explained. Diversity is the main motivation
for the paper as it is mentioned in the abstract but any theoretical or empirical work to support it is
completely missing.
- Motivation and methodology including the steps to evaluate generated samples is understandable but
some parts are ambiguous (please refer above comments).
- Based on the current state of the experiments, the contributions don’t seem to be significant as there
is not enough validation to support the claims.

 typos:
- Line 43 : Instead of ”Figure 1 (b)”, it should be Figure 1 (a)
- Line 168 : Section 3.3 First letters of all the words in the heading must be capital
Other remarks :
- Line 56 : Instead of ”If the model lacks essential attributes” the following sounds technically
correct ”if model lacks ”representation” of essential attributes”.
- Line 56-58 : This claim does not seem to be correct.
- Line 147 : Link to the specified figure is missing
- Figure 2 is never refered to in the text, is it an unnecessary figure?
- Line 253 : Generated set has non-smiling men and non-smiling women. But Figure 4 caption
says otherwise

Questions:
1. If I understood correctly, the text and the image encoders are different? If yes, then
the embedding space of both models is going to be very different and will be influenced by the
downstream tasks that they are trained on. Would this metric always work for embeddings from
different models? Does it make sense to calculate similarity between embeddings from different
models?
2. Table 1 represents accuracy results for CS vs DCS. What is meant by evaluating ”positive
samples” in line 164 and 165? And what is meant by ”how well they are aligned”? Also, what
exactly is the accuracy calculated for?
3. In Section 3.1, there is a mention of ”each channel of the embedding being utilized”, how exactly
does this happen?
4. Line 196-197 claims that the generated distribution is desirable only if it has exactly same number
of samples as in the training set that has a particular attribute. Why should it be? Afaik, the
underlying distribution of latents represent semantic features of the data and the generative
model is responsible to generate novel samples that could be realistic but not necessarily show
exactly same features same number of times in the training set. So, is the assumption in the
mentioned lines appropriate? Can you prove this or maybe refer to some papers that provide
such guarantees?

Limitations:
limitations have been addressed

4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.

title: Residual Scheduling: A New Reinforcement Learning Approach to Solving Job Shop Scheduling Problem

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a deep reinforcement learning-based constructive heuristic to solve the (Flexible) Job Shop Scheduling Problem. An instance of the problem is represented as a graph and fed into a Graph Neural Network-based model which outputs a score for each candidate (operation-machine) pair. The model is trained with the REINFORCE algorithm with as baseline a classic Priority Dispatching Rule-based heuristic. The novelty of the paper lies in the update of the state after each action: the graph is updated by removing the operations which have already been executed to focus on the most relevant information, which is the residual operations and remaining times of the ongoing ones. The proposed approach is experimentally shown to outperform RL-based constructive heuristics on classic JSSP and FJSSP benchmarks.
reviews.strengths:
1. Sound and interesting idea of removing irrelevant information from the state 
1. The paper is fairly clear and I appreciated the illustrations Fig 1-3.
1. The model was proposed for the JSSP and easily adapted to the Flexible JSSP
1. The approach outperforms deep RL-based construction heuristics on classic benchmarks
reviews.weaknesses:
1. Limited novelty: the main contribution is the definition of the residual state at each step of the construction process by removing irrelevant operations and resetting the time reference. This seems to me an incremental improvement of the approach L2D [1], which already proposed a similar state graph representation and the use of the Graph Isomorphism Network architecture for the JSSP.
1. The proposed approach seems very specific to (F)JSSPs (state representation, baseline) and it's not clear what could be transferable to DRL heuristics for solving other optimization problems.
1. In the experiments, the presented non-learning-based baselines seem pretty weak: only greedy (see more in Questions)
1. Lots of English typos (I noted a few per page)

[1] C Zhang et al, Learning to Dispatch for Job Shop Scheduling via Deep Reinforcement Learning, Neurips 2020
reviews.questions:
1. Where dos the attributes of edges (L205) appear in the GNN model (Sec 3.3)?
1. The paper claims: “Interestingly, using RS, the average gaps are nearly zero for the collections with sizes larger than 100 … A strong implication is that our RS approach can be scaled up for job sizes and even reach the optimal for sufficient large job count.” Do you have an idea of why the model would work better on unseen large instances versus instances of the same size as the training ones? Does OR-tools return the optimal solutions for these larger instances? Can it be that the quality of the reference solutions decreases and therefore the “optimality” gap becomes smaller?
1. Are there stronger non-learning-based baselines for the JSPP other than the greedy PDR heuristics presented in Table 2? To be able to appreciate the performance of the proposed approach, it would be useful and more convincing to compare it to the best heuristics for this problem, beyond simple greedy ones. For example maybe [2]?
1. Appendix, Algorithm 1, Line 6:  to compute this makespan at state s_t, given action a_t, do you do a rollout of the current policy \pi_{\theta} with the updated parameter \theta? This would mean doing a rollout until the end of both the baseline and current policy at each step of the trajectory? How long did the training take?
1. How is the average computation time computed? (Table 13) In particular, was the policy applied to each instance individually or were instances batched? 
1. The discussion L322 about the time it takes for RS/L2D/ScheduleNet versus OR-Tools can be a bit misleading: OR-tools is an exact solver. If spends a lot of time proving the optimality of the solution. Probably it could return a good quality solution much faster if used as a heuristic. In addition it runs on CPUs and not GPUs therefore just comparing the time is not really fair. 

[2] CY Zhang, A tabu search algorithm with a new neighborhood structure for the job shop scheduling problem, Computers & Operations Research, 2007
reviews.limitations: Not explicitly addressed by the paper.
